articles.article_language: To see what sum of money the Town will vote to raise and appropriate, transfer from available funds, or otherwise provide for the payment of unpaid bills of previous years, incurred by the departments, boards and officers of the Town of Natick; or otherwise act thereon
articles.discussion: Mr. Townsend presented this article. He shared that the appropriation will be sourced from State Aid in an amount just under $60,000. He shared that we budget our State Aid number based on the Governor’s initial estimates. However, State Aid exceeded the initial estimate by over $200,000, and is the funding source for this Article. He noted that there is a bill for the golf course in this Article, which we don’t usually do for Enterprise funds. The Administration decided to fund that invoice through this Article, and make an adjustment in the Indirects calculations. Motions & Debate Members asked various questions regarding the specific unpaid invoices and funding: Does the town have a credit account with Amazon business? Mr. Townsend said yes, they do take our purchase orders - and confirmed that these are unpaid invoices. 46 A member noted that there are identical amounts for the same vendor - are they actually two different invoices? Mr. Townsend said yes, they did confirm that, it’s just a recurring charge that is the same each month. A member asked whether the date of the Sentinel invoice is the date of the service provided or the bill date. Mr. Townsend said it was the bill date. A member asked about one of the bills for software (“Brightly) and whether that was an annual bill or a one time expense? Bill Spratt, Executive Director of the Department of Public Works and Facilities, explained that this was a one time fee for consulting and project management for implementing the software. That was a year-long project. The issue with this bill was that the software itself is approximately $66,000/year, but they had already paid that. So, there was some confusion about what this bill was for which led to a delay, which is why it has ended up on this list out of the fiscal year. The software itself is typically paid out of our IT budget but the project management expense would have come out of DPW. A member asked where the rest of the FY2026 State Aid would be going. Mr. Townsend said it was being reserved for Spring but Mr. Errickson added that it could be used for other things this session as it comes up. NOTE: See Recommendation Book Appendix for invoice detail. The committee voted 13-0-0 to recommend Favorable Action on the subject matter of Article 4.

articles.discussion: This motion was presented by Town Administrator Jamie Erickson. He explained that total new growth is currently expected to be $2,050,000 and this appropriation approximately represents the difference between budgeted new growth ($1,100,000) and expected actual new growth. As of the printing deadline for the Finance Committee recommendations book, FY26 new growth had not yet been certified. If FY26 New Growth is not certified prior to the Town Meeting session at which this Article is considered, or if such New Growth is certified at an amount insufficient to meet the proposed appropriation, Town Meeting should expect an amended motion. In response to Finance Committee member questions, Mr. Erickson stated that (i) the proposed appropriations of FY26 new growth had been reviewed by the Assessor’s office and (ii) the proposed Operation Stabilization Fund would bring the balance in such fund up to the level of the Town’s target. The committee voted 13-0-0 to recommend Favorable Action on the subject matter of Article 5.

articles.discussion: Town Administration explained that this is the semi-annual appropriation request to transfer funds remitted to the town, by the three cable companies licensed to operate in Natick. These are the fees collected from the end-consumers, held in a segregated account until appropriated, and transferred to Natick Pegasus. Reminder: The traditional cable subscription business model is “evolving”. Consumers are cord-cutting, and moving away from paying cable subscriptions - including the fees that currently fund Natick Pegasus. In light of this long term trend, the town needs to consider and plan for a different funding source for Natick Pegasus. The committee voted 13-0-0 to recommend Favorable Action on the subject matter of Article 6.

articles.article_language: To see what sum of money the Town will vote to raise and appropriate, transfer from available funds, borrow or otherwise provide to implement a Capital Improvement Program as may be required for capital equipment for the various departments of the Town of Natick; to protect the physical infrastructure of the Town of Natick, to add new physical infrastructure, or to improve community assets; and further to determine whether this appropriation shall be raised by borrowing or otherwise; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: Jon Marshall, Deputy Town Administrator for Operations, presented the capital plan. Mr. Marshall explained that the capital request is lighter than might be expected for a number of reasons, but the primary one is that the town is engaged in a comprehensive financial 56 planning process to evaluate how they will fund capital projects going forward, particularly those that are funded through borrowing (to ensure we have the recurring funds to pay for borrowing costs). That analysis will be integrated into the development of the five year capital improvement plan in the spring. In the meantime, the town is focused on only those projects that are the most urgent. Other key notes from the Finance Committee and Capital Subcommittee hearings: There is no request for additional money for the roadway improvement plan because they are able to fully fund all the projects they intend to do this season through existing state money (chapter 90). We are still comfortably within the borrowing capacity recommended by the Massachusetts Department of Revenue. However, the hesitancy to borrow more at this time is more about managing our debt service budget. Inflation continues to be an issue, especially on certain items. For example, the town looked back at its 2021-2026 capital improvement plan and noted that there was a 59% difference between the projected costs of the kinds of vehicles we need to purchase now and their quoted cost. The committee voted 13-0-0 to recommend Favorable Action on the subject matter of Article 9, with respect to both Motion A and Motion B. 57 Motion A (Borrowing) 58 59 Motion B (Capital Stablization) 60 61 62 63

articles.article_language: To see if the Town will vote to appropriate, borrow or transfer from available funds, an amount of money to be expended under the direction of the School Building Committee for the Memorial Elementary School building project, currently located at 107 Eliot Street, Natick, Massachusetts, for which feasibility study the Town may be eligible for a grant from the Massachusetts School Building Authority. The MSBA’s grant program is a non-entitlement, discretionary program based on need, as determined by the MSBA, and any costs the Town incurs in connection with the feasibility study in excess of any grant approved by and received from the MSBA shall be the sole responsibility of the Town; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: Dr. Melissa Spash, Superintendent of Natick Public Schools, presented this article along with Elise Gorseth, Chair of the School Committee, Karen Ghilani, Principal from Memorial School, Matt Gillis, Deputy Superintendent for Finance, Stephen Meyler, Chair of Memorial School Building Committee, and Jamie Errickson, Town Administrator. The purpose of this article is to fund a feasibility study and schematic design for a future Memorial Elementary School building project. The request for $2,600,000 would be funded through new borrowing after discussion of funding options with the Town Administration. Natick Public Schools were invited into the eligibility process with the MSBA in December 2024, and a feasibility study is the next step in the process. Approval of Article 10 would provide funding for modules 3 and 4, and allow the Town to complete a feasibility study and schematic design (see MSBA Process & Approvals as well as MSBA Building Process Overview). The funding would allow the Memorial School Building Committee to define a preferred solution, develop reliable cost estimates, and bring a full project proposal to the community. Dr. Spash also explained that Natick’s window to secure this funding and stay on track with the MSBA application deadline is about to close on October 31 (270 days after being invited to Module 1). Natick is seeking an extension, but it is not likely to be a very long one. Favorable Action by Fall Annual Town Meeting 2025 is essential to keeping on track for the MSBA process. The current projected timeline includes a debt exclusion vote in 2027 - 2028 before construction could begin. MSBA Process & Approvals 66 MSBA Building Process Overview The MSBA invited Natick into the eligibility phase based on four criteria: ● Elimination of existing severe overcrowding ● Prevention of severe overcrowding expected to result from increased enrollment. ● Replacement, renovation, or modernization of school facility systems, such as roofs, windows, boilers, heating and ventilation systems, to increase energy conservation and decrease energy related costs in a school facility ● Replacement of or addition to obsolete buildings in order to provide for a full range of programs consistent with state and approved local requirements. 67 Supporting examples for each criteria were included in the April 2024 Statement of Interest, which was the basis of MSBA’s invitation to the eligibility phase. Overcrowding and Inadequate Space for Programs: The school was built for 312 - 348 students but currently serves 440 - 450 students. Hallways and closets are being used as instructional spaces, Pre-K has had to be moved off-site, and Memorial cannot support a full special education / english language learner program. There is inadequate storage for afterschool programs, and core spaces for Art, Music, Dining, Gym, and Library are undersized for the current population. Aging and Failing Building Systems: Electrical system is old and undersized, windows, doors, and masonry are deteriorating with leaking and other issues. The building is not ADA compliant, and the front office has no view of the school, which can be a safety issue. Loss of Core Educational & Community Functions: Playground equipment near end of life, current building cannot support preschool, full special education and multilingual learner programs, flexible spaces and cafetorium are being used as classrooms and small group spaces. Dr. Spash outlined how the proposed project aims to create a modern elementary school for 600 learners, a neighborhood preschool, a full special education program, and space for multilingual learners, and adequate core spaces. The MSBA sets the final enrollment and reimbursement rate and Natick will receive the enrollment from MSBA in the next phase.Based on their current estimates, the reimbursement rate for this project would be 45%. Mr. Gillis outlined the sub-costs of the feasibility and schematic design funding. The funding would cover hiring an Owners Project Manager, architectural plans, geothermal test wells, and consulting (and also a little contingency buffer). The $2,600,000 figure is based on other communities as a reference point for coming up with these amounts. Regarding funding sources, Jamie Errickson, Town Administrator, explained that free cash would not work with the October 31 funding deadline, which is likely before the Town will have certified free cash. Additionally, the Town generally spends free cash on more immediate projects. While the Town does have $4.9 million of unspent funds borrowed for the Kennedy Middle School project, these can only be reappropriated for projects of a similar useful life. Since the Kennedy Middle School borrowing was for a useful life of 20 years, it cannot be used for a feasibility study, which has a useful life of 5 years. New growth would not be sufficient to cover the requested appropriation for Article 10. Therefore, new borrowing is the preferred method of funding Article 10. The authorization would not necessarily be borrowed right away, so the Town would do short term borrowing, only making interest payments over a couple of years, allowing us to keep our interest costs down until we can wrap the 2.6 million into the larger, longer-term borrowing for the construction project after design work is done. Questions from the Committee A member asked how many Johnson Elementary students were added to the Memorial School population, and Dr. Spash confirmed that 60 students went to Memorial after the Johnson school closure. A member asked if the other elementary schools were at or over capacity. Dr. Spash answered that most were at capacity, though several schools have modular classrooms which are being used beyond their recommended useful life and this was of interest to the MSBA when considering Natick’s Statement of Interest. There are also 5 preschool classrooms at the East School because they cannot fit in the existing elementary school spaces. Every elementary school has had to get creative with providing small group or interventionist spaces in hallways, offices, and shared spaces. 68 Dr. Spash confirmed that while the building is over capacity, it is important to stress that the building is obsolete. It was built in 1966 and is now seriously deteriorating. To the extent there is overcrowding, a lot of it is at the pre-school level. But, more fundamentally, even though enrollment isn’t growing, the nature of our student population is changing in ways that impact physical space; for example, more special needs children and English language learners means that we need more space for small group instruction. A member asked about possible increases on the reimbursement rate, but these are all based on different design options, and is a more appropriate question for the Memorial School Building Committee during the feasibility study and design process. Increased reimbursement rates have historically been based on energy efficient designs, renewable energy sources, and past maintenance history. The Finance Committee members expressed support for the Article and gratitude to the presentation team, and had little debate. Some members expressed concerns about design outcomes or financial implications of varying reimbursement rates, which are relevant to future stages of the project. Finance Committee, Town Meeting, and the Natick community will hear more details about this project as it progresses. The committee voted 13-0-0 to recommend Favorable Action on the subject matter of Article 10.
articles.motion_text: Move that the Town of Natick appropriate the amount of Two Million Six Hundred Thousand ($2,600,000) Dollars for the purpose of paying costs to conduct a feasibility study for the Memorial Elementary School building project, currently located at 107 Eliot Street, Natick, Massachusetts, including the payment of all costs incidental or related thereto, and for which the Town of Natick may be eligible for a grant from the Massachusetts School Building Authority ("MSBA"), said amount to be expended under the direction of the School Building Committee. articles.article_language: To see if the Town will vote to update or amend the table of Select Board Appointments in Bylaw Article 10, Select Board; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: This motion was presented by Select Board member Richard Sidney. Mr. Sidney explained that the Select Board believes that the current table of Select Board appointments is outdated and includes some boards and/or committees that are no longer needed and which the Select Board is not otherwise required (by state law, Town Charter, Town Bylaws or intermunicipal agreement) to make. The motion is not intended to limit the scope of the Select Board’s appointments, but to list only those appointments about which the Select Board has no discretion. The Select Board could then continue to make (or cease to make) discretionary appointments without coming to Town Meeting to amend the table. A Finance Committee member noted that the motion omitted a committee that is required by 72 state law. In response to a Finance Committee member, Mr. Sidney stated that the table and motion had not been reviewed by Town Counsel. The Finance Committee voted to continue the hearing to a later date to allow for corrections to be made. At the continued hearing, a revised motion was presented. A Finance Committee member asked if any of the committees being removed from the current table were created by Town Meeting. Mr. Sidney stated that he was not certain. Another Finance Committee member noted that several of the citations to Massachusetts General Law in the revised motion were incorrect. A Finance Committee member expressed concern that the table of appointments only includes as ‘required’ those appointments established under state law, Town Charter, Town Bylaw and intermunicipal agreement, but does not include those appointments (if any) established by direct vote of Town Meeting. Several Finance Committee members considered the aim of the motion to have merit, but that the revised motion still had errors and appeared not to have had sufficient review. articles.article_language: To see if the Town will vote to amend or repeal Bylaw Article 27, Information Systems Advisory Board; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: Richard Sidney, Select Board member, presented this article. He explained that this is part of a review of all the boards and committees that the Select Board appoints. As part of that effort, they realized that the Cable Advisory Board (CAB) and the Information Systems Advisory Board (ISAB) have been co-appointed for at least the last 8 years. The Select Board wants to adjust the charge and description of a combined board. Because the Information Systems Advisory Board is in by-law, re-writing that charge is difficult and requires Town Meeting action to ratify. The Select Board wants to keep a combined board in existence, but they want to take it out of the by-law. Multiple Finance Committee members expressed various levels of concern about this proposal. Most of the concerns centered on the fact that boards and committees not specifically required in the bylaws can be sunset by the appointing authority without additional process or Town Meeting input. Multiple members expressed concern that the importance and complexity of information systems and technology is only increasing, and that a group of professionals volunteering their time and advice is of value. One Fincom member stressed the importance of having more input on critical functions, like security, not less. Members discussed the logic of having (most) members of the ISAB overlap with the CAB. 76 The main function of the Cable Advisory Board is to negotiate the contract terms of the three cable licenses operating in town - and then advise the Select Board as license granting authority. This is an infrequent task - which does have some limited technology and networking components. Motions and Debate: Motions were made and seconded to: 1- recommend Favorable Action, and 2 - Refer the article back to the Sponsor. Supporters of Favorable Action argued that there wasn’t a downside to removing the ISAB from the bylaws, and that it makes the process of modifying the charge and composition of the Board much simpler. Another proponent argued that if it’s not required by statute, it should not be in the bylaws. Supporters of Referral argued that Information Systems are critical and complex, and that weakening the requirement for having an advisory board is ill advised. The motion for referral passed by a vote of 10-2-0. For reference: Current Bylaw Article 27 – TO BE REPEALED: ARTICLE 27 INFORMATION SYSTEMS ADVISORY BOARD Section 1 Powers and Duties There shall be an Information Systems Advisory Board which shall advise the Town on policies, procedures, priorities, and planning of the information systems of the Town. Section 2 Composition, Term of Office Said Board shall be composed of seven (7) members who shall be appointed by the Select Board from within the Town and shall not be full-time employees of the Town. When available, a minimum of four (4) of said seven (7) members appointed by the Select Board shall possess knowledge and experience in the field of information systems and services. Appointees to the Information Systems Advisory Board shall serve for three (3) year terms commencing July first. In the event of a vacancy occurring in the membership other than by the expiration of the term of a member, the Select Board shall within sixty (60) days after a vacancy occurs appoint a successor to serve for the balance of the unexpired term. Section 3 Meetings Meetings of said Board are to be held at least four (4) times each calendar year. The committee voted 10-2-0 to recommend Referral to Sponsor on the subject matter of Article 12.

articles.article_language: To see if the Town will vote to accept the provisions of Massachusetts General Laws Chapter 41 Section 110A; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: Text of M.G.L. Chapter 41 Section 110A Section 110A: Office hours on Saturday Section 110A. Any public office in any city or town may remain closed on any or all Saturdays as may be determined from time to time, in a city by the city council, subject to the provisions of the city charter, or, in a town, by vote of the town at a special or regular town meeting, and the provisions of section nine of chapter four shall apply in the case of such closing of any such office on any Saturday to the same extent as if such Saturday were a legal holiday. Discussion and Questions: Andrew Ghobrial, Town Clerk, presented the article. He explained that this law, if adopted by the town, would allow the Clerk’s Office to close on Saturdays as we see fit. It would not impact the ability to hold a public hearing or a meeting on Saturday if it so chose. Once it is accepted, it would allow the Clerk's Office to close on certain Saturdays before Town 78 Elections. He noted three elections since he started - and during those three Saturdays, they saw hardly any traffic in the Clerk’s office. Nobody came in before the spring town election despite it being a high profile election due to the override. As he sees it, this is a waste of resources. He estimates the town spent at least $1,500 for that day. The current law in effect dates back to when residents would have to go to town hall to register to vote, but you can register in so many different ways now, such as online. A member asked to clarify whether this was about the weekend before the election or the last weekend before the deadline to register. Mr. Ghobrial answered that it was the latter. The member further asked whether this would allow us to keep the Clerk’s office open on Saturdays if we chose to do so. Mr. Ghobrial said that we would continue to have that option, this just means we don’t have to do that. Several members questioned the wording of the M.G.L. Section being adopted, and whether it was actually limited solely to the Clerk’s office. Mr. Errickson explained that he had an extensive conversation with Town Counsel, who has said this is the proper way to adopt this provision, including the flexibility to close the Clerk’s office on those days without other action or impact on other town offices or functions. Members asked how this change, and the office’s closure would be communicated to the community in advance. Mr. Ghobrial indicated that it would become part of the published “Political Calendar" published on the website. Members encouraged additional forms of notice which might better serve the users. Motions & Debate: Motion made and seconded to recommend Favorable Action. Multiple members felt that the underlying section of M.G.L. being adopted is badly written, and is unclear - with one arguing that adopting it is “collaborating with the Commonwealth in terribly written law.” Another member felt that adoption appears to make the town less available to the public. The Motion to recommend Favorable Action passed 8-0-4. The committee voted 8-0-4 to recommend Favorable Action on the subject matter of Article 13.

articles.article_language: To see if the Town will Amend Bylaw Article 50, Section 16.15 Dog License and Kennel License Fees; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: This motion was presented by Town Clerk, Andrew Ghobrial. Mr Ghobrial explained that this motion would remove the dog license and kennel fees from the Town Bylaws so that the Select Board would be able to adjust such fees from time to time without Town Meeting needing to change to the Bylaws, but that no changes to the fees were being sought at this time. Finance Committee members asked about the required process to change the fees if this motion were to pass. Mr. Ghobrial stated that no hearing would be required, but if the Select Board were to consider changes to the fees, it would do so only in a public meeting, as required by the Open Meeting law. 80 In response to a Finance Committee member, Mr. Ghobrial stated that the fees for unlicensed kennels were intentionally omitted from the scope of this motion as those fees are enforced by the police through the Animal Control Officer. Several Finance Committee members indicated their view that this would be helpful in streamlining the current onerous process to revise such fees, but expressed some concern that a streamlined process would carry some risk that less awareness of proposed changes would result. The committee voted 12-0-0 to recommend Favorable Action on the subject matter of Article 14.

articles.article_language: To see what action(s) the Town will take to establish a Hybrid Town Meeting Committee, in accordance with Section 2-11 (e) of the Town of Natick Home Rule Charter, to study and recommend a framework for conducting hybrid town meetings, to determine how the committee shall be constituted, to see what sum of money the Town will vote to raise and appropriate, transfer from available funds, or otherwise provide for the operation of the committee; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: The Town Moderator appeared at the hearing for this article. The Moderator surveyed Town Meeting members following the 2025 Spring Annual Town Meeting. 28% of respondents indicated that they would attend remotely or consider remote attendance. Medical issues were cited by many of those as a motivating reason. Finance Committee members inquired about the timeline and about the composition of the study committee. The Moderator stated that the absolute earliest that a hybrid town meeting could occur if there is a positive recommendation is 2026 Fall Annual Town Meeting, but that later was possible and may be likely. Some Finance Committee members were concerned that outreach be conducted to include people who do not participate in Town Meeting or people who may be skeptical about a hybrid Town Meeting. The Moderator had already considered the challenge of reaching out to people who are not among the highly active participants in town government and who may be reluctant or otherwise uninclined to seek appointment. The Moderator intends to seek a variety of perspectives. Other questions were about other towns that conduct hybrid meetings. The Moderator indicated that several towns conduct hybrid meetings, both representative and universal. He said that Plymouth may be the most notable. There are a few vendors whose systems support operation of a hybrid town meeting, including the vendor that Natick currently uses to operate its electronic voting system. The committee voted 13-0-0 to recommend Favorable Action on the subject matter of Article 15.
articles.motion_text: Move: WHEREAS, the Town of Natick recognizes the importance of maximizing voter participation and engagement in its democratic processes; WHEREAS, the advent of technology offers new opportunities to facilitate broader access to Town Meeting proceedings while maintaining the integrity and deliberative nature of the meeting; WHEREAS, it is deemed prudent to establish a committee to thoroughly research, analyze, and propose a comprehensive plan for the implementation of hybrid town meetings in Natick. 82 Therefore, it is hereby moved that the Town of Natick establish a committee to be known as the "Hybrid Town Meeting Committee" with the following charter: 1. Purpose: The Hybrid Town Meeting Committee shall be charged with the responsibility of studying and recommending a comprehensive framework for the conduct of hybrid town meetings in Natick, including but not limited to:  Reviewing the hybrid meeting practices currently in place at Representative Town Meetings in Massachusetts.  Evaluating various technological platforms and solutions for remote participation.  Developing protocols and procedures to ensure secure and verifiable remote voting.  Addressing issues related to accessibility and equity for all participants, regardless of their technological capabilities.  Proposing amendments to existing bylaws or the Town Charter as necessary to facilitate hybrid meetings.  Considering the logistical and financial implications of implementing hybrid meetings.  Recommending best practices for maintaining the decorum and deliberative nature of Town Meeting in a hybrid format. 2. Composition: The Hybrid Town Meeting Committee shall consist of nine (9) members, appointed as follows: Eight (8) Town Meeting members appointed by the Moderator. One (1) member representing Natick Pegasus appointed by the Moderator. 3. Reporting: The Hybrid Town Meeting Committee shall submit its recommendations to the Select Board and Town Meeting at the 2026 Spring Annual Town Meeting. The report shall include a detailed proposal for the implementation of hybrid town meetings, including any proposed bylaw amendments or other necessary actions. 83 4. Funding: The Town shall appropriate the sum of $10,000 from FY 2026 State Aid to support the work of the Hybrid Town Meeting Committee, including but not limited to, the procurement of legal counsel, expert consultations, technological assessments, and public outreach efforts. Sunset Clause: Unless otherwise extended by a vote of Town Meeting, the Hybrid Town Meeting Committee shall cease to exist upon the submission of its final report or by June 30, 2026, whichever comes first.

articles.article_language: To see if the Town will vote to accept Jennifer Circle as a public way, and any appurtenant easements thereto, as laid out by the Select Board and as shown on a plan or plans on file at the Department of Public Works and the Office of the Town Clerk; To see if the Town will vote to authorize the Select Board to acquire by gift, purchase, eminent domain or otherwise, easements in any land necessary for laying out and acceptance of Jennifer Circle, and any appurtenant drainage, utility or other easements related to said Jennifer Circle and/or accept grants thereof; and, further, to authorize the Select Board and other applicable Town of Natick boards and personnel to take all related actions necessary or appropriate to accomplish the purposes of this article; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: Mr. Errickson, Town Administrator, presented this article. He explained that this is a roadway that is seeking to be accepted as a public way. This is a small subdivision that was constructed in the 90s, but it was never accepted at that time as a public way. The Select Board adopted standards for accepting roadways. The residents on Jennifer Circle went through the standard process and the road scored very well on that process. The Select Board has endorsed the advancement of Jennifer Circle through the program. Motions & Debate A member asked why the roads presented this Town Meeting for acceptance are not public roads already. Mr. Errickson said it was different for each Article. Usually what happens is a developer builds a subdivision but then needs to submit the roadway to the town for acceptance but that doesn’t always happen. It isn’t clear why that happened with Jennifer Circle. A member asked if the people on unaccepted roads have snow plowing and other town services. Mr. Errickson said yes, that they are plowed for public safety purposes, though there are a few exceptions to that. A member asked if the residents of Jennifer Circle had already requested that work be done on the road. Mr. Errickson said that was correct. Another member asked what roadwork needs to be done. Mr. Errickson said it isn’t specific but putting the road into the roadway maintenance program so that it can be assessed and maintained as part of that program, including proper crack sealing up to repaving. A member asked whether the town has a bond associated with work required of this or other subdivisions. Mr. Errickson said he did not know. Continuing, the member asked whether, by accepting this, the town would lose the surety the town has or can the town claim that? Mr. Errickson said based on the record, they didn’t find a record of a surety or bond associated with this. A member asked how many houses were on this street. There are 5-10 houses in it. The committee voted 11-0-1 to recommend Favorable Action on the subject matter of Article 16.

articles.discussion: Claire Rundelli, Conservation Agent, presented this article. This would create a revolving fund for funds that are received for related to stormwater management. The Stormwater Management bylaw was created in 2012, and funds have been collected according to its provisions. These funds can’t be utilized until an authorized revolving fund is established. This action will be followed up by a subsequent authorization to put money in the fund in the spring. 90 A member sought clarification on the source of the funds already collected, as well as funds going forward. The Sponsor explained that the funds come from applications for activity permitted under the Stormwater Management bylaw. To date, approximately $170,000 has been collected - most within the last 1.5 years. These collected funds will be transferred to the newly established revolving fund next spring. A member asked, and the Sponsor confirmed that these will not be classified as restricted funds, and that opinion has been reviewed by Town Counsel. The committee voted unanimously (13-0-0) to approve the motion to Recommend Favorable Action. The committee voted 13-0-0 to recommend Favorable Action on the subject matter of Article 19.

articles.discussion: Ms. Loomis, Director of Community and Economic Development noted that the Planning Board unanimously supported this. It updates an existing bylaw that was designed to prevent the stripping of land for sale outside of the town. The proposed change would allow the removal of certain things that cannot be reused after certain types of projects. Motions & Debate Motions were made and seconded for both recommendations for Favorable Action, and for Referral to the Planning Board. Proponents of Favorable Action noted that the proposed changes have been reviewed by Town Counsel, the Planning Board, and CED. The changes make sense and are appropriate to update the existing bylaw. Proponents of Referral to the Planning Board noted that, despite previous review, there was an error found [and updated], so some members questioned the need for additional review. Some members also noted that the subject matter could have been presented earlier in the meeting/hearing calendar, which would have increased the likelihood of catching errors and adjustments. The committee voted 5-8-0 to reject the referral motion. The committee voted 8-5-0 to recommend favorable action on the subject matter of Article 20. The committee voted 8-5-0 to recommend Favorable Action on the subject matter of Article 20.
articles.motion_text: Move that the Town vote to amend Section V-F Removal of Earth Products of the Natick Zoning Bylaws by 5. deleting the words “Building Inspector” after the words “… except that the removal of said material may be permitted subject to the issuance of a building permit by the …” and before the words “and only to the extent of excavating for the building foundation…” and replacing with the words “Building Commissioner”; 6. deleting the second paragraph which reads “Pre-existing use of premises for the excavation or removal of sand and gravel can be continued and extended throughout the premises. articles.article_language: To see if the Town will vote to amend the Natick Zoning Bylaws by amending § V-G Flood; and further amend any other associated sections, where applicable; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: This motion was presented by Director of Community and Economic Development, Amanda Loomis. Ms. Loomis explained that the purpose of this motion is to delete a bylaw that is not currently being utilized and which would prohibit construction anywhere in Natick because of how broadly it is written. She noted that the Flood Plain bylaw and the Stormwater Management bylaw are intended to address the same concern. One Finance Committee member noted that zoning bylaws are written stating limits on what property owners can do on their property, but that the reason for such restrictions is to protect abutting property owners. In response to a Finance Committee member question about how (if such bylaw were deleted) a property owner would be protected against an abutting neighbor’s use of property subject to flooding in a manner as to endanger health or safety of 94 the occupants, Ms Loomis stated that if the abutting property was covered by the Stormwater Management bylaw or was in a flood plain district, then Stormwater Management and/or Flood Plain bylaws would be the source of protection. One Finance Committee member stated that if this bylaw is not currently being utilized, then deleting it should not change things for the Town. Other Finance Committee members expressed concern that the intent of the bylaw proposed to be deleted may not be fully addressed by the Stormwater Management bylaw and the Flood Plain district bylaw and that such deletion could leave people exposed whose properties are not in the designated district where the Stormwater Management bylaw applies or in a flood plain district. The committee voted 9-4-0 to recommend Referral to Sponsor on the subject matter of Article 21.

articles.article_language: To see if the Town will vote to amend the Natick Zoning Bylaws by amending Section 200 – Definitions for Limited Site Plan Review; and further amend any other associated sections, where applicable; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: Ms. Loomis, Director of Community and Economic Development, presented this article. These changes are a requirement of the MA Attorney General for the Limited Site Plan Review bylaw, to clarify our compliance with the Dover Amendment. Members reviewed the AG’s letter to the Town on this matter, and questioned if the AG’s opinion was a directive that changes are necessary, or if only recommended. The Sponsor indicated that the AG is requiring the changes, and that Town Counsel concurred. Subsequent to the Finance Committee hearing, Town Counsel affirmed that opinion. Motions & Debate: Motions were made and seconded for both a recommendation of Favorable Action, and for Referral to the Planning Board. Members supporting Favorable Action noted that it’s the opinion of the Planning Board, CED, and Town Counsel that these changes are required by the AG’s office, and make sense for full compliance with the Dover Amendment - which imposes limits on local review of certain projects brought forward by institution such as schools, religious organizations, qualified day cares, etc. Members supporting Referral, expressed uncertainty that the changes are really required;. A member also noted that the provisions related to day care had previously been in a different part of state law, and was unsure that they should be incorporated into these changes. The committee voted 5-8-0 to reject the Referral motion. The committee passed the Favorable Action motion by a vote of 8-5-0 The committee voted 8-5-0 to recommend Favorable Action on the subject matter of Article 22.

(Art. 23, 2024 Spring ATM, 05/09/2024) . 96

articles.article_language: To see if the Town will vote to amend the Natick Zoning Bylaws by amending § III-A.2 Use Regulations Schedule (Use Categories within the Use Headers: G. Motor Vehicle Related Sales and Service Uses; H. Transportation, Communication, Utility Uses; and K. Manufacturing and Industrial Uses (amended to K. Light Manufacturing Uses; and N. Industrial Uses); amend Section 200 – Definitions; and further amend any other associated sections, where applicable; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: Ms. Amanda Loomis presented this article. This is a continuation of the clean-up and clarification of the Regulation and Use tables. While that project isn’t yet 100% complete, it’s getting close. In addition to clarifications and better usability, a major goal is to align Natick’s bylaws to the latest case law. Member Questions: A member asked for clarification on the clean-up of residual “Reserved” notations, for items previously deleted via prior clean-ups. Ms. Evans, chair of the Planning Board, replied that they are confident at this point in the project that it’s safe to do so, and that the notations are no longer needed. A member asked about the location of a specific definition, and Ms. Loomis indicated that it’s elsewhere in the Zoning Bylaw. 108 Several members asked about the timing of this specific article’s hearing. What prevented it from being available for review/study way earlier in the hearing calendar? Ms. Evans explained that the ordering of the Planning Board’s work had been discussed, and that there wasn’t traction for making changes that would have distributed the review copies of the article earlier, or allowed it to be scheduled at an earlier date. It’s an issue that will get continued attention. A member asked what other communities do with regard to the Planning Board to Town Meeting cycle. Ms. Loomis explained that it varies, but that there are opportunities for Natick to improve how it schedules work - including the Finance Committee hearing phase. Motions & Debate Moved and seconded a motion to recommend Favorable Action. That motion passed by a vote of 10-1-0. The committee voted 10-1-0 to recommend Favorable Action on the subject matter of Article 23.

articles.article_language: To see if the Town will vote to amend the Natick Zoning Bylaws by creating a new Neighborhood Corridor (NC) Zoning District and amending the existing Limited Commercial (LC) Zoning District, including amending Section 200 -Definitions; deleting Section 107 Purpose of Limited Commercial (LC) District; adding Neighborhood Corridor (LC) to § II-A.2; add a new § II-C Zoning District Purposes; adding a new § II- C.1 Limited Commercial (LC) Zoning District and § II-C.2 Neighborhood Corridor (NC); amending § III-A.2 Use Regulation Schedule; deleting § III-D Use Regulations for LC Districts; adding a new § III-D Neighborhood Corridor (NC) Zoning District; amending § IV-B Intensity Regulations for the Limited Commercial (LC) Zoning District; adding a new § IV-C Intensity Regulation for Neighborhood Corridor; amending § V-D.3 for off- street parking; amending § V-J.3 Inclusionary Housing; amending § VI-DD Special Permit and Site Plan Review; amending § VI-K Design Review Board; amending or acting in any other manner in relation thereto. Further, amend the Natick Zoning Maps by creating a new Neighborhood Corridor (NC) Zoning District; amending the Limited Commercial (LC) Zoning District, all as showing on the maps on file with the Town Clerk’s Office; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: Amanda Loomis, Director of Community & Economic Development and Terri Evans, Planning Board chair, presented this article. Ms. Loomis explained that this project area is now called the “West Central Corridor" as a better description. This primarily deals with the area along 135 in West Natick just south of the railroad tracks. This area is currently broken up into multiple zoning districts. The Planning Board first tried to better understand what was going on in the corridor. Once that was completed, the Board proposed creating several subdistricts that allows each parcel to be treated individually based on its actual use. This is important here because of the varied topography in that area. Additionally, Ms. Loomis explained that there was a significant amount of input from property owners in the area - and they had recently sent out 1,200 legal mailers. The input from property owners nearby emphasized the need for more walkability and helping small businesses. The presentation walked the committee through each subdistrict, explaining its characteristics and components. There is a subdistrict designation specifically for Conservation and Open Space. The town’s Comprehensive Master Plan, Natick 2023, focused on this area as a major opportunity for Mixed-use – especially in association with the West Natick train station. This zoning change requires contributions to a multi-purpose path on the north side of 135 and requires connection between parking lots to cut down on curb cuts, as well as connectivity between alleys. The plan allows for site plan review for the commercial 125 projects in this area, for which the Planning Board would be the review authority. The is a smaller component of this article that deals with a small area located on Rt 27 north of Rt 9. Four properties on Lenox St sit on lots which are split zoned. A strip of their lots is currently zoned Limited Commercial (LC), and are being rezoned Residential Single A (RSA). A small strip of Lowell Road, to the centerline is being rezoned from Commercial-II (C-II) to Limited Commercial (LC). Questions, Motions, and Debate: A member asked about community engagement, and suggested that it had been insufficient to this point – having recently missed sending out updates to residents who had signed up. Ms. Loomis explained that there had been staff turnover, and that the Communications Director has been engaged. Members had questions about parking requirements, and how they will be calculated. Ms. Loomis said that an applicant would present a proposal based on industry standards and similar projects. Then, the Planning Board would review that and get a peer review consultant if necessary. In her experience, applicants generally provide adequate parking, because it is difficult to lease a space without it. A member asked about noise abatement, especially considering that increased building size along the railroad track might reflect more noise towards residential areas than currently happens. Ms. Loomis explained the requirements for both hardscaping and landscaping as mitigation. The member asked about the MBTA installing noise barriers, which the Sponsor said they would look into. A member asked about the tree planting requirements, and some of the spacing details. Ms. Loomis described the process, and explained that the spacing is specified as a range, because exact spacing requirements would run into issues with driveways, utilities, etc. A member asked about the target density in the various subdistricts. Ms. Loomis directed the committee to the tables that define the specifications by district. The member had a further question about the need for a Conservation and Open Space designation. It was explained that it’s hoped that additional properties can be those areas, and having specifically designated areas will facilitate that. Questions & Comments from the Public Mr. Brian Lucenta, Natick resident, asked about the expected population increase, and the impact on the schools and town infrastructure. He asked whether the Superintendent of Schools or the School Committee has had input into this. Ms. Loomis said that they have not – although they go under the assumption that the children would be dispersed throughout the schools and grade levels without a major impact. The resident had a similar question about the Fire Department. Ms. Loomis explained that the Fire, Police, and Public Works departments had all participated in the planning. Mr. Lucenta explained that he graduated 126 High School in 2004, and that, in his view, lots of other housing units have been added in Natick since that time. He argued that this is going to raise costs for the schools significantly and we should take a pause before adding more housing. Members moved and seconded a Referral to the Planning Board on Article 24 on Motions A and B. Members also moved and seconded a recommendation for Favorable Action on Motions A and B. Members supporting Referral argued that the lack of notice to people who attended the summer 2024 meeting meant that there should be a further meeting to give people opportunities to attend and be engaged. Also there are people on Boden Lane who are extremely concerned about this development because of the traffic flow that this will create. They also noted the impact on the schools and town infrastructure. Another member expressed concern about the short time provided to review and study the documents. Several minor corrections were made, and he’s not confidant that additional time wouldn’t have provided additional needed corrections. A different member noted he thinks that the proposed subdistrict approach makes sense in this area, and that a lot of good thought had gone into the proposal – but that he feels that additional work is required. Members advocating a Favorable recommendation felt that, while not perfect, the proposal is very solid. One member noted living in the general area for years, and that the area is only getting worse. Improvements in more bike and pedestrian connectivity is a great aspect of this article, and it is an opportunity to have a revenue dense area in a place that is currently very vacant. The Referral Motion failed 5-6-0. The committee voted 6-5-0 on the Favorable recommendation. Since Natick bylaws require a minimum of 8 votes for a recommendation, the committee has No Recommendation on Motions A and B, On Motion C: The committee saw the value of this minor change, and voted 11-0-0 to unanimously recommend Favorable Action. The committee has No Recommendation on Motions A and B, and voted Favorable Action the subject matter of Motion C of Article 24.

West Central Corridor (WCC) and Subdistrict The West Central Corridor (WCC) Zoning District is a single district made up of 昀椀ve (5) subdistricts. Each subdistrict re昀氀ects its location along the corridor, its relationship to surrounding neighborhoods, and the desired scale of development. Boundaries are intended to blend gradually rather than create sharp edges, and each subdistrict has tailored standards for building height, intensity of use, and design to promote coordinated, compatible development across the corridor. a. Subdistrict 5, West Central Hub (S5) The West Central Hub Subdistrict (S5) functions as the corridor’s primary mixed-use center. It o昀昀ers the highest permitted building height and density in the WCC District and is located for convenient access to transit 115 and other transportation options. Development in S5 shall create a walkable, pedestrian-oriented environment with active street-level frontages that accommodate a mix of uses, including retail, dining, o昀케ces, entertainment and recreation, civic and educational facilities, and housing. b. Subdistrict 4, West Central Connector (S4) The West Central Connector Subdistrict (S4) serves as the corridor’s link between the Hub (S5), other subdistricts, and nearby neighborhoods. It accommodates mid-scale mixed-use development and is intended to be accessible by both vehicles and non-motorized travel. Development in S4 shall maintain active, pedestrian-friendly street frontages and may include a mix of commercial, service, and residential uses. c. Subdistrict 3, West Central Residential (S3) The West Central Residential Subdistrict (S3) focuses on predominantly residential development at a small- to mid-scale. It provides a transition in height and building form between the mixed-use corridor (S4 and S5) and adjacent low-density neighborhoods, while maintaining pedestrian connections to nearby subdistricts and open spaces. d. Subdistrict 2, West Central Single Residential (S2) Reserved e. Subdistrict 1, West Central Conservation and Open Space (S1) The West Central Conservation and Open Space Subdistrict (S1) preserves natural resources and open land while allowing limited landscape improvements for active recreation, passive enjoyment, and habitat restoration. Development is restricted to trails and supporting amenities. Most S1 land is held or protected by the Commonwealth of Massachusetts, the Natick Conservation Commission, or a non-pro昀椀t land trust. 2. Use Regulations a. A Lot in the WCC Zoning District may include two (2) or more uses as identi昀椀ed in § III-A.2 Use Regulation Schedule of these Zoning Bylaws. b. All projects with a residential component shall c omply with § V-J Inclusionary Housing of these Zoning Bylaws. c. All projects S5 and S4 are required to include an a ctivated ground 昀氀oor by incorporating uses that encourage and support pedestrian activity, the Subdistrict, and abutting neighborhoods. i. Minimum Non-Residential Requirement for Residenti al Mixed-Use Developments a) Buildings with a residential component in S5 shall dedicate at least 昀椀fty percent (50%) of the ground-昀氀oor frontage facing a public way, or 昀椀fty percent (50%) of the building’s longest façade—whichever is greater—to non-residential uses. The designated non-residential space shall have a minimum depth of twenty-昀椀ve (25) feet. Up to twenty percent (20%) of that non- residential area may be used for residential-support amenities such as a lobby, leasing or management o昀케ce, co-working space, community room, or 昀椀tness center. b) Buildings with a residential component in S4 shall dedicate at least forty percent (40%) of the ground-昀氀oor frontage facing a public way, or forty percent (40%) of the building’s longest 116 façade—whichever is greater—to non-residential uses. The designated non-residential space shall have a minimum depth of twenty-昀椀ve (25) feet. Up to twenty percent (20%) of that non- residential area may be used for residential support amenities such as a lobby, leasing or management o昀케ce, co-working space, community room, or 昀椀tness center ii. The Planning Board shall con昀椀rm the required non -residential square footage at the opening public hearing for Site Plan Review and shall recon昀椀rm that 昀椀gure at the close of the hearing if building plans are amended during the review. The required non-residential area may be provided in a single building or distributed among multiple buildings on the same lot. 3. Intensity, Scale, and Design Regulations a. A Lot within the WCC Zoning District may have mor e than one (1) Building or Structure per Lot. b. The WCC Zoning District shall comply with § IV-B.3 Intensity Regulations of these Zoning Bylaws. c. T o facilitate smooth transitions between subdistricts, properties in S4 or S3 may apply the Intensity Regulations in § IV-B.3 to up to twenty percent (20%) of the portion of the lot that directly abuts another parcel in S5, S4, or S3. This 昀氀exibility is intended to allow a gradual change in building scale and bulk. Building-height limits may not be increased under this provision except by special permit from the Planning Board. d. Design Standard Requirements Projects within the WCC Zoning District shall be designed to ensure coordinated building placement, streetscape and landscape treatments, and compatible massing at subdistrict edges, while meeting the standards in § III-D.3 of these Zoning Bylaws. e. Pedestrian Scale and Design Intent Projects within the WCC Zoning District shall be designed to create a safe, attractive, and comfortable pedestrian environment along the public street frontage and within on-site walkways and open spaces. i. Sidewalks and street frontages shall be sized, lo cated, and improved to accommodate the anticipated level of pedestrian activity. ii. Buildings and structures shall be placed to main tain a consistent street frontage and reinforce a walkable public realm. iii. Projects shall provide pedestrian-supportive am enities—such as benches, lighting, landscaping, bicycle racks, and way-昀椀nding elements—that bene昀椀t both site users and adjoining neighborhoods. f. Major Transportation Corridor Design Intent Projects within the WCC District that front on West Central Street, Speen Street, or Mill Street shall be designed in coordination with the Department of Public Works (DPW) on matters such as driveway and curb- cut locations, sidewalk and streetscape design, drainage, and utility connections, and shall be consistent with applicable transportation plans to reduce vehicle trips and improve access to transit, pedestrian access, and bicycle facilities. 117 i. Street frontages shall be designed to encourage p edestrian activity and provide safe access to bicycle facilities, seating areas, pocket parks or open spaces, and similar streetscape features that support a walkable environment. ii. Projects shall incorporate alleyways or pedestri an pathways, where feasible, to connect with adjoining properties and reduce local vehicle trips. If a physical connection cannot be established at the time of construction, the project shall reserve and clearly identify a future connection point to enable such linkage when adjoining properties redevelop. iii. Projects shall coordinate the location and desi gn of bus stops, shelters, or other transit-supportive amenities with the appropriate transit agency and DPW to facilitate convenient use of the public- transportation network. iv. Non-residential and Residential Mixed-Use Develo pment projects with frontage on West Central Street, Mill Street, and Speen Street shall support non-vehicular modes of transportation through the design and construction of a multi-use pathway or sidewalk along the frontage of said project(s). g. O昀昀-street Parking and Garages Intent In the WCC Zoning District, o昀昀-street parking shall be treated as a secondary, supporting element of the development rather than a dominant site feature. i. O昀昀-street parking garages and surface parking are as shall be located to the side or rear of buildings so that primary street frontages remain pedestrian-oriented. ii. Where a garage is located to the side of a build ing, its street-facing wall shall be set back so that it extends no more than ten (10) feet forward of the building’s front façade. iii. All o昀昀-street parking lots shall be built with conduit and reserved space for transformers and switchgear to accommodate future installation of electric-vehicle (EV) charging stations in at least 昀椀fty percent (50 %) of the total parking spaces. h. Architectural Design Intent Projects within the WCC District—including Residential Mixed-use Development, non-residential, and Multi-Family Dwelling buildings—shall be designed to: i. Break down the apparent mass of large or mid-scal e buildings through fenestration patterns, façade articulation, and other architectural techniques. ii. Incorporate varied window styles, materials, and façade details that contribute to a pedestrian-scaled street frontage. iii. Where a building includes podium parking that f aces a public street and has no ground-昀氀oor commercial use, the street-facing façade shall be treated with architectural elements—such as glazing, active lobby space, or display windows—to maintain an activated street frontage. i. Landscape and Open Space 118 Projects within the WCC District shall incorporate landscape and open- space elements that enhance the site design and provide transitions to neighboring properties. i. Provide a combination of landscape features—such a s plantings, berms, walls, or fencing—to bu昀昀er the project while creating a gradual transition to adjacent properties. ii. Plant a minimum of one (1) shade tree for every twe nty-昀椀ve (25) to thirty (30) linear feet of the building footprint. iii. Plant a minimum of one (1) street tree for every t wenty-昀椀ve (25) to thirty-昀椀ve (35) linear feet of street frontage (excluding curb-cut widths) within the required front-yard setback. iv. Where o昀昀-street parking spaces required by the Z oning Bylaws are not initially needed at the time of construction, the unused areas shall be reserved and may be improved as pervious pocket parks or open-space areas until such parking is required. v. Where a lot directly abuts the MBTA Commuter Rail corridor, the project shall install and maintain a planted or landscaped bu昀昀er— using a combination of vegetation, hardscape, and soft-scape materials—along the rear property line. The vegetated bu昀昀er shall be a minimum of ten (10) feet in width, located within the rear- setback requirement of § VI-C. articles.article_language: To see if the Town will vote to amend the Natick Zoning Bylaws by amending §III-M Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU); amending Section 200 – Definitions Accessory Dwelling Units; or otherwise act thereon
articles.discussion: Ms. Loomis presented this article, and explained that the changes are required by the Attorney General’s office. In August 2024, a MA law was passed requiring ADUs as a right under Massachusetts law. In response, Natick developed and passed its ADU bylaw that fall. In the spring of 2025, the town passed an amendment to the definition of Gross Floor Area, based on earlier guidance from the AG’s office. Questions: A member asked of the existing bylaw is in effect, in abeyance because of required corrections, or partially in force. Ms. Loomis explained that is partially in force. Multiple members questioned certain aspects of ADU placement on a lot, especially as it relates to setbacks and height. Ms. Loomis explained that the current bylaw manages height and proximity differently than the AG’s revised requirements – and that ADUs must be of a height and placement matching the limitations of the primary structure to which they are accessory. This is less “neighbor friendly” than the bylaw Natick originally developed – but is one of the required changes. Multiple members were quite adamant in their objections to having the issue dictated by the state. They stated that they are unable to vote in favor of an action they feel is coercive. A member moved to amend the motion, to add “or Bus Station” to the multiple places “Commuter Rail Stations”. The amendment impacts the requirements to provide a parking space. During discussion of the amendment, various concerns were debated: What is the definition of “Bus Station”? Does it require a permanent building? A passenger shelter? How does the MWRTA’s routes that operate by people waiving down the bus impact implementation? Multiple members felt that since the addition of “Bus Stations” was referenced in the AG’s letter, it should be addressed in the revisions. The amendment passed (6-5-0). NOTE: since the Amended Motion ultimately received “No Recommendation” from the Finance Committee, the motion(s) presented here are the originals as submitted by the Sponsor. A motion was made and seconded to recommend Favorable Action, The motion received a vote of 6-5-0. Since the Natick bylaws require a minimum of eight votes for any recommendation, the committee has No Recommendation on Article 25. The committee voted 6-5-0, and has No Recommendation on the subject matter of Article 25.
articles.motion_text: Move that the Town vote to amend Section 200 – De昀椀nitions of the Natick Zoning Bylaw, by deleting all references to “Principal One-Family Dwelling” and replacing with “Principal Residential Dwelling” in all occurrences for the De昀椀nitions of Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU), Gross Floor Area, to read as follows: Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU): per MGL c. 40A §1A, an Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU) is a self-contained Residential Dwelling Unit, inclusive of sleeping, cooking, and sanitary facilities on the same lot as a Principal One-Family Dwelling, subject to otherwise applicable dimensional and parking requirements, that: (i) maintains a separate entrance, either directly from the outside or through an entry hall or corridor shared with the Principal One-Family Dwelling su昀케cient to meet the requirements of the state building code for safe egress; (ii) is not larger in 昀氀oor area than half (1/2) the 昀氀oor area of the Principal One-Family Dwelling or nine-hundred (900) square feet, whichever is smaller; and (iii) is subject to such additional restrictions as may be imposed by a municipality, including, but not limited to, additional size restrictions and restrictions or prohibitions on Short-term Rental of ADU, as de昀椀ned in section 1 of chapter 64G, as further de昀椀ned herein; provided, however, that no municipality shall unreasonable restrict the creation or rental of an accessory dwelling unit that is not a Short-term Rental. (Art. 20, Fall ATM, 10/24/2024) 128 Gross Floor Area: The sum of the areas of all stories of a building measured from the exterior faces of the exterior walls or from the centerline of walls separating two buildings, including any 昀氀oor area below grade when usable for residential, o昀케ce, business, storage, industrial, or other purposes, but excluding any area used exclusively for heating, air conditioning or other mechanical equipment which services the building, and excluding 昀氀oor area intended or designed for o昀昀-street parking. The Gross Floor Area of a Principal One-Family Dwelling or an Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU), shall be calculated per 760 CMR 71.00, as amended. ~ (Art.28, Spring ATM, 05/06/2025) Move that the Town vote to amend Section 200 – De昀椀nitions of the Natick Zoning Bylaw, by deleting the second sentence “A Family Suite was de昀椀ned as an accessory dwelling unit, in a principal One-Family Dwelling, for occupancy by a resident who is related by blood, marriage, or adoption to the owner of the principal Residential Dwelling.” and replacing with a new sentence “A Family Suite was de昀椀ned as an accessory dwelling unit, in a Principal One-Family Dwelling for occupancy by a resident who is related by blood, marriage, or adoption to the owner of the Principal Residential Dwelling.”, to read as follows: Family Suite – See Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU). Prior to February 3, 2025, Family Suites were permitting in the Single Residential (RS) Zoning Districts (RSA, RSB, and RSC) by special permit from the Zoning Board of Appeals (ZBA). A Family Suite was de昀椀ned as an accessory dwelling unit, in a Principal One-Family Dwelling for occupancy by a resident who is related by blood, marriage, or adoption to the owner of the Principal Residential Dwelling. (Art. 20, Fall ATM, 10/24/2024) And further move that the Town vote to amend Section 200 – De昀椀nitions of the Natick Zoning Bylaw, by deleting the de昀椀nition “Dwelling, Principal One-Family” and replacing with a new de昀椀nition “Dwelling, Principal Residential”, to read as follows: Dwelling, Principal Residential: For the purposes of § III-M Accessory Dwelling Units (ADU), the Principal Residential Dwelling will be the primary use, while the ADU will be the accessory use on the Lot. The ADU may be attached or detached to the Principle Residential Dwelling. (Art. 20, Fall ATM, 10/24/2024) And further move that the Town vote to amend Section 200 – De昀椀nitions of the Natick Zoning Bylaw, by deleting the de昀椀nition “Principal One-Family Dwelling” and replacing with a new de昀椀nition “Principal Residential Dwelling”, to read as follows: Principal Residential Dwelling: See Dwelling, Principal Residential (Art. 20, Fall ATM, 10/24/2024) 129 And further move that the Town vote to amend § III-M Accessory Dwelling Unit, by amending §III-M.1, III.M.2.d, III-M.3.a, III-M.3.b, III-M.3.e, III.M.4.a.i, III.M.4.a.ii, III.M.4.a.iii, III-M.4.b.i, III-M.4.c.i, III-M.4.d.i, III.M.5.c, III.M.5.e, and III.M.5.g by deleting “Principal One- Family Dwelling” and replacing with “Principal Residential Dwelling” in all occurrences; amending §III-M.3. by deleting §III-M.3.c in its entirety and renumbering the remainder of the subsection (“d.” will be the new “c.”, “e” will be the new “d.”, and “f.” will be the new "e.”); deleting § III-M.5.d in its entirety, and renumbering the remainder of the subsection (“e.” will be the new “d.”, “f.” will be the new “e.”, “g.” will be the new “f.”, and “h.” will be the new “g.”); amending Section III-M.5.e by deleting the words “a minimum of” that occur after “An ADU shall provide” and before the words “one (1) o昀昀-street parking space on the Lot when located a half (1/2) mile or more from a commuter rail station.”, to read as follows: Section III-M. Accessory Dwelling Unit 1. Purpose and Intent An Accessory Dwelling Unit (ADU) is an alternative type of housing that allows Natick to expand and diversify its housing supply options without requiring additional land development. The design and implementation of an ADU is expected to 昀椀t on a Lot, with the Principal Residential Dwelling. 2. Massachusetts General Law (MGL) a. ADUs are permitted As of Right per Massachusetts General Laws (MGL), Chapter (c) 40A, § 3. b. The Town has established §III-M of these Zoning Bylaws to provide guidance and to reasonably regulate the construction of ADUs within single-family zoning districts. c. No ADU unit may be utilized as Short-term Rental. d. The gross 昀氀oor area of the Principal Residential Dwelling or the ADU outlined in § III- M of these Zoning Bylaws shall be calculated per 760 CMR 71.00, as amended. (Art. 20, Spring ATM, 05.06.2025) 3. Applicability and Requirements a. In order to qualify for an As of Right ADU, the Principal Residential Dwelling and the Lot shall: i. conform to the maximum Building Coverage of the respective Zoning District, per § IV-B Intensity Use Regulations of these Zoning Bylaws; and ii. be the only residential Dwelling Unit on the Lot prior to construction of the ADU. b. Neither the Principal Residential Dwelling nor the ADU shall be used as a Short-term Rental. c. Where there are two (2) or more ADUs on a Lot. One (1) of the ADUs shall be deed restricted as a昀昀ordable per and subject to § V-J Inclusionary Housing of these Zoning Bylaws. 130 d. The ADU and the Principal Residential Dwelling shall remain on the same Lot and shall not be divided onto two (2) separate lots. e. No ADU unit may be separately sold as a condominium, or otherwise. 4. Permitting Regulations and Permit Review Authority a. Building Permit Review – Building Permit Application The Building Commissioner shall be the permit granting authority for the creation of the following types of ADUs. Such review shall be conducted concurrently as part of a Building Permit application. i. A single ADU that is located entirely within the Principal Residential Dwelling or within an existing attached garage, with no exterior increases in square footage; ii. A single ADU located in an existing detached garage that is greater than ten (10) feet from the Principal Residential Dwelling. Detached garages less than ten (10) feet from the Principal Residential Dwelling shall meet all Building Code requirements; iii. A single ADU to be located within a Principal Residential Dwelling at the time of construction of the Principal Residential Dwelling; or iv. The conversion of a Family Suite to an ADU, unless such conversion requires relief from the Zoning Board of Appeals (ZBA), and then either section c. or d. shall apply, depending on the facts and circumstances. Consultation with Town sta昀昀 is encouraged before any conversion application is 昀椀led. b. ADU Site Review (As of Right) The Building Commissioner shall be the permit granting authority for the creation of the following types of ADUs. ADU Site Review shall be As of Right and limited to the bulk, height, scale, and placement of a single ADU on the Lot. i. A single detached ADU on a Lot located within the Allowable Building Envelope and greater than ten (10) feet from the Principal Residential Dwelling; ii. The construction of a single ADU on a Lot that is de昀椀cient only in Lot Area and Frontage; or iii. The construction of an addition for a single ADU that does not require relief from the ZBA. c. ADU – Pre-existing Non-conforming Lots (As of Right) The ZBA shall be the permit granting authority for ADU Site Review, which is an As of Right (majority-vote) review. The review of the following ADUs shall be limited to the bulk, height, scale, and placement of a single ADU on the Lot. i. The expansion of an existing Principal Residential Dwelling or existing building (that is greater than ten (10) feet in distance from the Principal Residential Dwelling) that is de昀椀cient in Intensity Regulations per §IV-B of these Zoning Bylaws; or ii. The placement or addition of an ADU requires a Section 6 Finding beyond relief associated with Lot Area and Lot Frontage. 131 d. ADU Special Permit The ZBA shall be the Special Permit Granting Authority (SPGA) for the following Special Permits, which shall require a super-majority vote: i. The creation of two (2) or more ADU per Principal Residential Dwelling or Lot, which will require a super-majority vote. ii. A Building proposed to exceed the allowable Lot Coverage or other dimensional requirements per § IV-B Intensity Regulations by Zoning District, with the exception of Lot Area and Lot Frontage. The Special Permit shall be required for the Building, not the ADU as a use. 5. Design Requirements and Findings The permit granting authority or SPGA shall review an ADU application for compliance with the following Findings. a. The ADU shall be located in the Allowable Building Envelope of a Lot. b. The Lot shall remain conforming to Building Coverage per §IV-B of these Zoning Bylaws for the respective Zoning District after the construction of an ADU. c. An ADU detached from the Principal Residential Dwelling shall be a minimum of ten (10) feet from another Structure on the Lot, per the State Building Code. d. A detached ADU shall not be located between a roadway and the designated frontage of the Principal Residential Dwelling. e. An ADU shall provide one (1) o昀昀-street parking space on the Lot when located a half (1/2) mile or more from a commuter rail station. f. When an ADU has a separate exterior entrance from the Principal Residential Dwelling or is a separate Building, the ADU shall be accessible from a path or walkway. g. The ADU is located on the Lot so it does not impede vehicular access and circulation of the Lot, roadways, and sidewalks. 6. Review Procedures The ZBA shall evaluate each application based on the following respective Review Criteria. a. ADU Site Review Procedures For uses outlined in § III-M.4.c of this Bylaw, the following public review procedures shall be followed. i. The ADU Site Review shall be posted on a public meeting agenda. ii. Notice of the public meeting shall be sent to abutters within three hundred (300) feet of the boundaries of the Lot. iii. A decision shall be rendered within thirty-昀椀ve (35) days of the 昀椀rst public meeting. b. ADU Special Permit Procedures For uses outlined in § III-M.4.d of this Bylaw, the review of such application shall comply with § VI-DD.1.e of these Zoning Bylaws. Art. 20, Fall ATM 10/24/2024 132 .

articles.article_language: To see if the Town will vote to amend the Natick Zoning Map by rezoning 0 Worcester Street (Assessor Map 23, Lot 69A) from Residential Single A (RSA) to Commercial – II (C-II) Zoning District; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: Questions and debate by committee members surfaced repeated concerns about the impact of this rezoning on the nearby residential neighborhood in West Natick. The lot coverage limit would increase from its current level of 25% to 30%. The building height limit would increase from 30 feet currently to 2.5 stories and 35 feet. Concern was expressed that the resulting move of the boundary of residential zoning would shift the buffer zones called for around the commercial areas that are subject to agreements between Natick and Framingham, and that the shift would move those buffer zones closer to existing homes in the neighboring portion of Natick. This concern was disputed but not resolved to the satisfaction of some committee members. There was also concern that the proposed new zoning could allow location of research laboratories in close proximity to residences. 134 The committee voted 9-4-0 to recommend Referral to Sponsor on the subject matter of Article 26.
