articles.article_language: To determine whether the Town will appropriate or reserve from the Community Preservation Fund annual revenues and prior year fund balance in the amounts recommended by the Community Preservation Committee for identified community preservation projects in Fiscal Year 2026, with each item to be considered a separate appropriation, in accordance with Mass. Gen. Laws c. 44B; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: David Krentzmen, Vice Chair of the Community Preservation Committee (CPC) presented these motions alongside Amanda Loomis, Director of Community and Economic Development. The Sponsor explained the CPC’s process. It starts with a letter of interest, then a review to see if it meets the criteria for spending CPA money on it. If it does, it goes through a lengthy application process, after which it is voted on by CPC members. Seven projects were presented, each individually having public hearings, discussion, and debate before the CPC. The CPC is simply recommenders – it is ultimately up to Town Meeting whether to fund each project. They feel very confident in the decisions they have made as a committee that these projects are worthwhile for the town. Motion A - Bacon Free Library Mr. Krentzmen explained that this is for the Bacon Free Library historic fence restoration. The idea is to restore the cast iron fence that surrounds the library property. This is in the historic preservation bucket and was a unanimous recommendation. The total project amount is $236,650. 139 Motions & Debate Mr. Jacobs, seconded by Mr. Coffey, moved that we recommend favorable action on the subject matter of Article 28, Motion A subject to the same language described above. The committee voted unanimously to approve the motion (10-0-0). Motion B - Housing for All Mr. Krentzman explained that this is a collaboration between two local non-profits and the Council On Aging. It is under the affordable housing “bucket” that is part of their purview. This was a 8-0 recommendation from the CPC. This is a two year request for $300,000 ($150,000 per year for 2 years) to provide rental assistance and security deposits to those in need. A member asked about the restriction of funds - if the funds go to Housing for All, what assurances are there that actual disbursement will be made in accordance with the terms of the application and vote? Ms. Loomis explained that if the project is ultimately funded by Town Meeting, the town and the applicant enter into an agreement which outlines how the funds are distributed and managed and on what timeline which town counsel would prepare. The member sought additional assurances, in the form of some instrument tying disbursement to substantial compliance with the conditions approved by the CPC at the time it voted approval of the project. The following solution was proposed and voted by the Finance Committee: Each Motion will have language added tying funds disbursement to substantial compliance with the conditions approved by the CPC at the time it voted approval of the project. That language has been added to each motion. 140 Motions & Debate Mr. Coffey, seconded by Ms. Monahan, moved that we recommend favorable action on the subject matter of Article 28, Motion B. Mr. Munnich made a motion, which was accepted as a friendly amendment, to include the reference to document title and date discussed above. The motion passed unanimously (10-0-0). Motion C - Study and Design for Morse Institute Library Mr. Krentzmen explained that this is a study and design project for the historic parts of the Morse Institute Library. The idea is to study existing conditions and design to guide future restoration efforts. This was recommended unanimously by the committee and is in the amount of $115,000. A member asked how projects internal to town administration (as opposed to agreements with outside entities) differ in their management. Mr. Marshall, Deputy Town Administrator, Operations said that instead of a contract, most towns do an MOU that serves a similar purpose. 141 Motions & Debate Mr. Jacobs, seconded by Mr. Munnich, moved that we recommend favorable action on the subject matter of Article 28, Motion C. The committee voted unanimously to approve the motion (10-0-0). Motion D - Memorial Beach Shade Structure Mr. Krentzmen explained that this is for a shade structure at Memorial Beach. This is for the purchase and installation of a third shade structure at Memorial Beach. The cost is $40,000 and was approved 8-0. A member asked whether this is a new structure or replacement of an existing structure. Town administration explained that this is for a completely new, separate structure - and that the two existing structures were coming to the end of their life, but those will be a capital project, not a CPA request. A member of the public noted that she doesn’t like this use of CPA funds. and feels like it should be capital funding. Further, she feels that this pond is becoming less and less usable because of bacterial accumulation and possible PFAS. 142 Motions & Debate Mr. Jacobs, seconded by Mr. Coffey, moved that we recommend favorable action on the subject matter of Article 28, Motion D. The committee voted 9-1-0 to pass the motion. Motion E - South Natick Park Improvements & Design Mr. Krentzman explained that this was for design and permitting work for the south natick parks around pleasant street. The amount of the project is $82,500. A member asked whether this area includes the riverbottom of the Charles River. Ms. Loomis said that to the best of her understanding, this is for the permitting and the design, so this is for what is out there today, not what will be after the removal of the spillway. Mr. Marshall added that this is for where the existing waterline meets the park areas. This will move from permit level designs that we already have, to construction level design and to move through the permitting process. The water will have to find its level and then there will be a future phase to deal with that but that’s out of scope for this proposal. 143 Mr. Munnich, seconded by Mr. Jacobs, moved that we recommend favorable action on the subject matter of Article 28, Motion E. The committee voted unanimously to approve the motion (10-0-0). Motion F - Preserving Natick’s Battle of Bunker Hill Muster Roll Mr. Krentzmen explained that this project is to preserve and digitize the 1775 Battle of Bunker Hill Muster Roll. This costs $10,000 and was recommended unanimously. Jeff Alderson, Town Moderator, asked whether the restoration would be completed in time for the 250th anniversary of the Nation’s founding. Niki Lefebvre, Director of the Natick Historical Society, explained that this is part of the plan. 144 Motions & Debate Mr. Jacobs, seconded by Mr. Munnich, moved that we recommend favorable action on the subject matter of Article 28, Motion F. The committee voted unanimously to approve the motion (10-0-0). Motion G - 5 Auburn Street Mr. Krentzmen explained that this is for historic preservation and the creation of affordable housing. This is for the conversion of that property to 32 units of affordable housing. The request is $750,000. The vote was 6-1-0 of the CPC. 145 A member asked why the number here seems to have increased. Sharone Small, Director of Real Estate and Metrowest Collaborative Development explained that the overall costs of the project have gone up because of hazardous materials found in the building, as well as increased material costs. The requests from the LOI to the application also went up because of the changes in certain tax credits. A member asked how receiving funding from the CPC would help obtain grant funding for this project. Ms. Small said that a showing of a local commitment to the project is a factor in the award of grant funding from the state. This funding represents only 3% of the total funding for this project and it would leverage approximately $30 in external funding per $1 of local funding. Another member asked how many more dollars of funding do the Sponsors anticipate asking for from the town. Ms. Small they have no plans to ask for more money as of now. A member of the public, asked whether this motion allows for the commitment but not the actual disbursement of funds until construction starts. Mr. Marshall explained that it would be through the contracting period that this would be figured out - but the conversations with staff are that funds would not be appropriated until they are needed. The member of the public further asked that we not fund something before the construction actually starts. The concern is that CPA funds will be disbursed and spent on early phases of the project, and if it doesn’t get completed, those funds will be lost without recourse. Mr. Marshall said the intent between the CPC and the applicant is that the funds wouldn’t be released to them until they got all of their project funding. Could the language be added to this motion? Yes, but I’m not counsel so I couldn’t say what that would say. A different member of the public, asked whether the CPA funding came from transfers of property or real estate taxes. Ms. Loomis explained that CPA funds are collected from local real estate taxes after the first $100,000 of value (1%) plus a state match. The member of the 146 public further said that including the assessed value of the property, plus the commitment of the Natick Affordable Housing Trust, Natick is up to nearly $6 million on this project. In the CPA documentation, it says that the local preference will only be consistent with state and federal law of 70%. She asked how this is calculated in terms of how many of the apartments are actually eligible. Ms. Small explained that the exact number of apartments will be finalized when they receive funding but before construction on the project starts. For that reason, it is subject to change, but generally funding given through a project based voucher is not allowed to get a local preference. So, some apartments could be ineligible for the 70% local preference. Motions & Debate Mr. Jacobs, seconded by Ms. Monahan, moved that we recommend favorable action on the subject matter of Article 28, Motion G subject to correcting the errors/additions discussed tonight. Mr. Coffey, seconded by Mr. Munnich, moved that we recommend referral to the CPC on the subject matter of Article 28, Motion G. Proponents of Favorable action argued that this is a once in a generation opportunity to increase our affordable housing stock significantly, and that we should support it. Projects like this, where we can leverage CPA funds to get multiples more in funding from outside sources, were one of the big selling points of the CPA. They argued that it is hard to think of a better value for our money - and further noted that this project has been through extensive processes in front of many other boards and committees and it would be unwise for the Finance Committee to attempt to derail it at this stage. Proponents of Referral expressed concern that this project is going to cost Natick more money in the future and this town is strapped for cash. $750,000 can go to a lot of other affordable housing projects and all these things need to be cleared up before they can feel comfortable that this is “ironclad good and absolute.” Another Referral supporter explained that he had previously been a major advocate for affordable housing. However, he has grave concerns about this project. There are fundamental mechanics of the town that have not been taken into account - such as the fact that the South Natick fire station can’t be replaced and the town is ignoring its obligation to do something there, but we are giving land away. That is a done deal, but now we are getting into the sunk cost fallacy as the numbers keep rising. This often happens with projects, but each time we do this, it’s an opportunity to re-assess this project. That member is unconvinced that the prior reviews were sufficient because nobody was looking at the big picture. A member explained that she supports this project and thinks the increased costs are understandable given the increased costs of materials. She also noted that the CPA has already collected multiples more money than this request and we are just getting started. Further there are no other affordable housing projects before us in this session and if you want to spend money on affordable housing, this is the way to do it. Moreover, this has nothing to do with the override, because those are different sources of money. The referral motion failed by a vote of 4-6-0 147 The positive motion also failed by a vote of 6-4-0 (per Natick bylaws, any recommendation of the Finance Committee requires 8 votes). The committee voted 10-0-1 (A, B, C, E, F) and 9-1-0 (D) to recommend Favorable Action and has No Recommendation on Motion G in the matter of Article 28.

Motion B Move that the Town vote to appropriate a sum of $150,000 per year for two consecutive years, in the total amount of $300,000 ($273,339.94 from the Community (Affordable) Housing Reserve Account and $ 26,660.06 from the Budgeted Reserve Account) for the purposes of funding application CPA-25-2 Housing for All, as recommended by the CPC (as presented in the documentation included in CPA Funding Request Application CPA-25-2 as voted upon by the Community Preservation Committee on September 8, 2025). Motion C Move that the Town vote to appropriate a sum of $115,000 ($115,000 from the Historic Preservation Reserve Account) for the purposes of funding CPA-25-3 Study and Design for the Historic Morse Institute Library (14 East Central Street), as recommended by the CPC. (as presented in the documentation included in CPA Funding Request Application CPA-25-3 as voted upon by the Community Preservation Committee on September 8, 2025). Motion D Move that the Town vote to appropriate a sum of $40,000 from the Open Space and Recreation Reserve Account for the purposes of funding CPA-25-4 Memorial Beach Shade Structure (63 West Street), as recommended by the CPC. (as presented in the documentation included in CPA Funding Request Application CPA-25-4 as voted upon by the Community Preservation Committee on September 8, 2025). Motion E Move that the Town vote to appropriate a sum of $82,500 from the Open Space and Recreation Reserve Account for the purposes of funding CPA-25-5 South Natick Park Improvements Design and Permitting (7, 11, 23 Pleasant Street), as recommended by the CPC. (as presented in the documentation included in CPA Funding Request Application CPA- 25-5 as voted upon by the Community Preservation Committee on September 8, 2025). Motion F Move that the Town vote to appropriate a sum of $10,000 from the Historic Preservation Reserve Account for the purposes of funding CPA-25-6 Preserving Natick’s 1775 Battle of Bunker Hill Muster Roll with $10,000, as recommended by the CPC. (as presented in the documentation included in CPA Funding Request Application CPA-25-6 as voted upon by 138 the Community Preservation Committee on September 8, 2025). Motion G Move that the Town vote to appropriate a sum of $750,000 from the Budgeted Reserve Account for the purposes of funding CPA-25-7 5 Auburn Street, as recommended by the CPC. (as presented in the documentation included in CPA Funding Request Application CPA- 25-7 as voted upon by the Community Preservation Committee on September 8, 2025). .

articles.article_language: To see if the Town will vote to amend the Natick Zoning Bylaws by amending § V-H Signage and Advertising Devices, 2. Downtown Mixed Use District (DM) and Center Gateway (CG) Signage; amending Section 200 - Definitions; amending § VI-K.4.d Signs and Advertising Devices; and further amend any other associated sections, where applicable; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: The article was presented by the Executive Director of the Natick Center Cultural District. This article arose in the spring of 2025 when it was determined that A-frame signs are not allowed in the Downtown Mixed-Use and Center Gateway districts in Natick Center. The Select Board granted a temporary special exemption to allow A-frame signs pending an amendment to the by-law governing signage. The Director of Community and Economic Development stated the governing goals as giving businesses the ability to promote their presence while maintaining the desired character of Natick’s Downtown district. Questioning and debate revealed broad support for businesses to be able to communicate their presence through signage in a manner that could contribute to the economic viability and success of businesses in Natick. The same discussion also focused on some concerns about a compressed timeline and a reactive process. One committee member expressed a desire to see more attention to distinguishing site review by the Planning Board from licensing by the Select Board, and not to create an onerous process for applicants that mixes the two. Redline/Strikethrough - Provided for reference only and not to be included in the motion V-H. SIGNS AND ADVERTISING DEVICES D. Regulations and Restrictions Appliable to Use District 2. Downtown Mixed Use District (DM) and Center Gateway District (CG) (a) Accessory Signs In Downtown Mixed Use District (DM) and the Center Gateway District (CG) accessory signs that comply with the provisions hereinafter set forth are permitted. All other accessory signs are expressly prohibited. (Art. 24, Spring ATM, 05/02/2023) 1. Location: The sign shall be a昀케xed to a building, except as here-in after provided. A sign attached to a building shall be securely a昀케xed to one of the walls of the building and it shall be parallel with and not project more than six (6) inches from the face of such wall and shall not project beyond the face of any other wall of the building. 2. Size: Letters in a sign shall not be more than two (2) feet in overall height, exclusive of ascenders and descenders, with the tot al area of the sign not to exceed thirty (30) square feet. (Art. 24, Spring TM, 4/13/2021) 157 3. Number: There shall not be more than one exterior sign for each business, except that if the business has a direct entrance into the business in a wall other than the business front, there may be a secondary sign a昀케xed to such wall, and if the business has a wall, other than the business front, that faces upon a street or parking area, there may be a secondary sign a昀케xed to such wall whether or not such wall contains an entrance to the business; provided however, that no business shall have more than two secondary signs in any event. The width of the secondary sign or signs shall not exceed one (1) foot in overall height or 昀椀fteen (15) square feet in total area. In addition to the foregoing sign or signs, there may be one directory of the occupants or tenants of the building a昀케xed to the exterior wall of the building at each entrance to the building. Such directory shall not exceed an area determined on the basis of one (1) square foot for each occupant or tenant in the building. 4. Standing Signs: Standing signs are prohibited. The committee voted 8-5-0 to recommend Favorable Action on the subject matter of Article 29.
articles.motion_text: Move that the Town vote to amend the Natick Zoning Bylaw by adding new definitions for Individual Building Establishment Unit; Multi-tenant Building; Sign, Awning; Sign Box; Sign, Blade; Sign, Building Identification; Sign, Directory Wall; Sign, Establishment Window; Sign, Exterior Notification Flag; Sign, Monument; Sign, Permanent; Sign, Projecting Hanging; Sign, Public Service; Sign, Roof; Sign, Sidewalk; Sign, Temporary; and Sign, Wall-Mounted, to read as follows: Section 200 – Definition Individual Business Establishment Unit: A single physical unit that is leased or owned by a commercial business or organization that is used for commercial business operations. Multi-tenant Building : A Building that contains more than one (1) Individual Business Establishment Unit. 149 Sign, Awning: A sign, also known as a canopy sign, located on the surface of an awning or attached above, below, or to the face of an entry canopy that identifies an Individual Business Establishment Unit or Multi-tenant Building. Sign Box: A predetermined, dimensional area of the first-floor exterior of a Building, as approved by the Planning Board, in which a business sign must be located for an Individual Business Establishment Unit or Multi-tenant Building. Sign, Blade: Similar to a Projecting Hanging Sign, but is a small, two-sided commercial sign that is attached to and projecting perpendicularly from the façade of a Building that identifies a commercial business located within an Individual Business Establishment Unit. Blade Signs may be stacked vertically. Information type is limited to the business name and logo. Sign, Building Identification : A sign that provides the recognized name of a Building, complex, or center, but not the name of a commercial business, Individual Business Establishment Unit, product, or service. Sign, Directory Wall : A sign that is located on an exterior wall, next to a publicly accessible entrance that is installed and maintained by a Multi-tenant Building owner to identify commercial businesses and their location within the Building. Sign, Establishment Window: A sign for a commercial business or organization that is located within an Individual Business Establishment Unit and is hung inside within three (3) inches or applied to the window itself. Such signage includes the name and/or the logo of the business but shall not include the advertisement for specific products or services. Sign, Exterior Notification Flag : A flag that notifies the public that a commercial business located within an Individual Business Establishment Unit is open or having a sale. Such flag shall be located on a pole that is no greater than four (4) feet in length, which includes the entire pole, bracket, and area where the flag is located. The pole shall be mounted to the exterior of a Building by bracket and not on the ground. Such signage shall only be displayed during hours of business operation and located as to not impede pedestrian mobility. Sign, Monument: A low-profile freestanding sign that is distinguished from other freestanding signs by its support structure. A Monument Sign is supported primarily by an internal structural framework or integrated into a solid structural feature, such as a masonry base or landscaping, rather than being held up by exposed poles. Sign, Permanent: A sign that is affixed to a Building, which includes the name and/or logo of a business(es) occupying an Individual Business Establishment Unit or name of the Building. Such signage does not advertise a specific product or service and does not include a phone number or website address. Sign, Projecting Hanging : A two-sided commercial sign that is attached to and projecting perpendicularly from the façade of a Building that identifies a commercial 150 business located within an Individual Business Establishment Unit. Information type is limited to the business name and logo. Sign, Public Service : A sign installed for the purpose of providing public information, wayfinding, or providing directions towards an indication of use not readily visible from the street (e.g., rest rooms, emergency services, etc.) and is managed by the Town of Natick. Sign, Roof: A sign that is erected on the roof or mounted to the uppermost horizontal architectural band of a Building to identify the name of the Building or commercial business. A Roof Sign is intended to be viewed by pedestrians and motorists from a distance and serve as an icon for the building. Sign, Sidewalk : Also known as a sandwich board or A-frame sign, is a portable, two- sided commercial sign that is placed on the sidewalk to identify a business or advertise daily specials or sales. Sidewalk Signs are intended to be viewed at close range by pedestrians on the same side of the street. Sign, Temporary : A sign that is removed daily. Sign, Wall-Mounted: A sign attached flat or mounted parallel to the facade of a Building that identifies a commercial business or organizational operations open to the public. And further vote to delete § V-H.D.2 Downtown Mixed Use District (DM) and Center Gateway District (CG) (sign regulations) in its entirety; and replace with a new § V-H.D.2 Downtown Mixed Use District (DM) and Center Gateway District (CG) (sign regulations), to read as follows: V-H.D 2. Information type is limited to business name and logo. 7. Awning Sign 154 1. Awning Sign may be located on the sloping portion of an awning are only permitted for storefronts where the typical area for a wall sign is missing. 2. Awning Signs are not allowed on the side of awning with closed ends. 3. Information type is limited to business name, logo, and address. 8. Sidewalk Sign 1. One (1) Sidewalk Sign is permitted per 昀椀rst 昀氀oor Individual Business Establishment Unit; or for use by non-昀椀rst 昀氀oor Individual Business Establishment Units, the owner of the Multi-Business Building is permitted to have one (1) Sidewalk Sign per publicly accessible entrance or one (1) per side of the Multi-tenant Building, whichever is less. 2. Sidewalk signs may be placed outdoors on-site or on a public sidewalk during business hours and must be removed when the business is closed. 3. Sidewalks signs displayed on a public sidewalk shall be placed in compliance with American Disabilities Act (ADA). 4. A sidewalk sign is not permitted to be illuminated or contain any electronic components. 9. Monument Sign 1. Only one (1) Monument Sign shall be permitted for a Building, complex, or center. 2. The structure of a Monument Sign shall consist of brick or masonry construction, complete with adequate footing. 3. The maximum height of a Monument Sign shall not exceed four (4) feet to the top of the sign above the ground 4. A Monument Sign shall be located a minimum of ten (10) feet from all property lines, and where deemed necessary by the Building Commissioner or the Planning Board, the setback shall be increased so that the sign and associated landscaping does not obstruct views from or onto other properties or site distance of a driver of oncoming, intersecting, or merging tra昀케c. 5. All Monument Signs shall be landscaped with well-maintained plantings ten (10) feet around the entire base of the sign. 6. Monument Signs are prohibited in the Downtown Mixed Use (DM) Zoning District. (d) Sign Maintenance 1. All commercial signs, including existing nonconform ing signs, together with any supports, braces, anchors, and ot her supporting hardware, must be maintained in good condition. 155 2. Damage to or deterioration of a commercial sign mus t be repaired within thirty (30) days of receipt of notice from the Building Commissioner/Sign O昀케cer. 3. When an existing commercial sign is removed, replac ed, or repaired, all supports, braces, anchors, and other supporting hardware that is no longer necessary must be remove d and any surfaces baring evidence of prior attachment must be repaired. (e) Permitting Procedures 1. Permanent Signage 1. Sign Box Permitting 1. The Sign Box is a permitted location that holds a Permanent Sign that is approved by the Planning Board by a simple majority vote. 2. Modi昀椀cations that increase the size, modify the shape, or modify the location of an approved Sign Box, but proposed new Sign Box complies with § V.H.2 of this Bylaw shall require a new review and approval by the Planning Board. 3. A Sign Box that requires dimensional relief or deviation of its location from § V-H.2 of this Bylaw shall require a Special Permit from the Planning Board. The Design Review Board (DRB) shall provide a letter of comment to the Planning Board for such Special Permit. 2. Established Sign Box Dimensions and Locations 1. Permanent Signs that are placed within an approved Sign Box require a Building Permit. 2. Changes to the content of a Permanent Sign that complies with an approved Sign Box shall require a new Building Permit before installation. 2. Temporary Signage a) The Select Board shall license Temporary Signage annually. b) A license shall include but is not limited to the location where a Temporary Sign can be located, requirements for use of a Temporary Sign, in addition to maintenance of condition of the Temporary Sign. 3. Public Signage 1. The Select Board shall be the approval granting authority for all Public Service Signs and Directory Signs that are installed within the public right of way or on Town property for the purposes of way昀椀nding. 2. Prior to review by the Select Board, the dimensions and location of Public Service Signs and Directory Signs shall be reviewed by the Police Department, Fire Department, Department of Public 156 Works, and the Building Commissioner to ensure that the signage does not compromise public safety and circulation. 3. Design associated with public way昀椀nding shall be developed through an open, public process.

articles.article_language: To see what actions the town will take to amend the Bylaws of the Town with regard to: Article 81 Chemical Storage Tanks and Systems of the ByLaws : To see what action the town will take regarding Article 11 Town Clerk of the ByLaws: 1) add “propane”, “underground propane tanks”, in Section 2a Applicability and elsewhere to provide for regulation of underground propane tanks and pads for above ground propane tanks to protect drainage areas, septic areas, wetlands and similar areas and 2) add exceptions for “propane” in Sections 6(e), 9(c), 10(a) or other sections to provide that such propane tanks not be subject to fuel monitoring, inventory control and reporting as may be required for chemical tanks and other underground or above ground fuel storage tanks; Article 22 Town Counsel and New ByLaw Section 3) “i) Add a new Article to the ByLaws consistent with Article 2 Section 11 (e ) of the charter and any other relevant provision of the charter or general laws i) to establish a standing committee for and or of town meeting to study review and report on zoning bylaw changes in advance of town meeting action and to determine the composition, method of appointment, term, powers and duties of such committee and/or ii) amend Article 22 of the ByLaws to require and to provide for the appointment of legal counsel with specialized knowledge in zoning and land use matters, to provide for the method of appointment, qualifications, powers and duties of such counsel including but not limited to advising the representative town meeting, advisory committees of town meeting, the planning board and/or town moderator; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: Motion A: Mr. Griesmer, Chair of the CBRC explained that last spring they were in front of the committee with a motion related to propane storage, but it had some issues that they had to fix so now they are bringing it back. This change would regulate the placement of such tanks underground by the Board of Health (BOH), which has an interest in regulating these to protect ground water etc. This change adds propane to the definition of “fuel” in the by-law that regulates underground fuel tanks to ensure that the BOH can regulate them. However, they don’t want to have propane tanks subject to the same regulations as oil and gasoline storage, so it is put in one section of the by-law but exempt from the rest of the by-law. A member asked what is being protected; is it about protecting against contamination from the fuel? Mr. Griesmer said that it is about protecting against contamination in part but the purpose language doesn’t preclude the by-law accomplishing other goals. Another member explained that water that can unnaturally pond because of these underground tanks, which can lead to contamination even if the contamination is not from the propane in the tank itself. The committee voted unanimously to approve the Motion A (13-0-0). 163 Motion B: Mr. Griesmer explained that this was also in front of the committee in the Spring. There were some fatal technical issues with that motion that required correction. This proposal would create an appointed town counsel specifically for zoning issues. The Sponsor explained that in his view, we need this because of the high volume of zoning by-laws that go on the warrant – an average of 14.5 a year. Zoning is quite complicated – to do good zoning, you have to have a good idea and concept, and it must be framed and drafted in words that are very technically refined and then inserted into the by-law in such a way that fits in terms of things like cross-references and definitions. If mistakes are made in a zoning by-law, someone can build a building that hangs around for 70-100 years, which is a serious consequence compared to other by-laws that are more easily fixed. Town Meeting members are not (generally) zoning experts, nor is the majority of the Finance Committee. The Sponsor further explained that we have had any number of really good zoning ideas that have had wording problems that qualify as outright errors. Some of them don’t get caught until there is a problem, while others get caught on Town Meeting floor - which isn’t ideal. With something this important, we need to provide a standing resource to the town who can assist anyone who wants assistance with this including the Planning Board, the Finance Committee, and Town Meeting. Some examples of things that, in the Sponsor’s opinion, were problematic: there was a marijuana zoning by-law that had dozens of errors in it which required multiple changes to fix; we had a cleanup of the Special Permit Granting Authority aspect of the by-law which put all districts on a list - but the Downtown Mixed Use zone inadvertently got left off the list. This led to three or more projects that were to be built without proper legal authorization; we had a cluster by-law that had so many issue we had to place a moratorium on it and then fix it; last year, Natick passed the ADU zoning by-law and half of it did not get approved by the AG; an error in the Special Permit procedures that were revised in 2024. While caught, there was an incorrect cross-reference which could have eliminated most requirements to get a special permit. Questions from the Committee: A member questioned the term of the appointment, and how differing appointing “windows” impact the ability of the primary and secondary appointing authorities ever to coordinate terms. The Sponsor indicated that partial term appointments can be made in order to synchronize future appointment terms and calendars. The general rule that legal counsel can only be appointed for single year durations also somewhat simplifies this coordination. Multiple members questioned the need for additional counsel outside Natick’s existing appointed Town Counsel firm. Current counsel has multi-disciplinary legal staff, and it seems simpler to ask for additional legal support from within existing counsel. The Sponsor indicated that there’s no reason that this additional, dedicated zoning counsel couldn’t come from within the currently appointed firm, but that it would need to be an additional, separate resource to get around bottlenecks and resource constraints. A member asked what prevents town administration from asking for an additional resource from the current town counsel’s firm. The Sponsor indicated that there’s nothing preventing that, other than funding. The intent of this bylaw change is to compel the hiring of additional zoning 164 specialty counsel, and to direct the administration to budget the additional appropriation needed. Multiple members asked questions related to costs of this change. The Sponsor indicated that it’s difficult to project, since it would be dependent on the amount of time budgeted, used, and the contract/retainer terms. Discussion continued trying to nail down an estimated budget, without additional clarification. A member asked what the future funding source would be, to which to Sponsor responded tax levy, as are the other budgeted legal line items. Multiple members questioned what discussions the CBRC has had with other stakeholders - such as the Planning Board, Select Board, ZBA, Town Administrator, etc. The Sponsor indicated that there has not been any recent discussions, and that most prior discussions indicated that the other stakeholders were not in favor of this proposal - sometimes strongly disagreeing with the reasoning and intent. Several members indicated a strong preference that this change be openly discussed with the various stakeholders, and that the outcome of those meetings be presented. Motions and Debate: There were two motions made, seconded, and debated. The first motion was for Referral to the Planning Board, Select Board, and Town Administration. The second motion was to recommend Favorable action. Debate supporting Referral centered on a couple of themes: Members felt that, while problems have certainly existed (some serious), the mechanisms already exist for engaging specialty zoning counsel - and additional bylaw changes are unnecessary. Many members also strongly feel that the process of engaging the other stakeholders and discussing the range of concerns is an important part of the process - and that it hasn’t happened. It was also noted that the intended outcome might not be realized if all stakeholders aren’t willing to engage/consult with this additional resource - and that it might be wasted funding. The lack of a firm range for the potential expense was also concerning. Members supporting a Favorable recommendation focused on this change as a potential solution to the long standing problem of errors, omissions, and drafting problems making it to Town Meeting deliberations - and in some cases, into the bylaws. These issues can cause serious, long lasting problems if implemented, and expend a very large amount of time and resources even when they don’t make it all the way to implementation. The time various boards, committees, and Town Meeting spends on corrective activity is of great concern. Voting: The Referral Motion failed 5-8-0. The Motion to recommend Favorable Action failed 6-7-0 The committee voted 13-0-0 to recommend Favorable Action on Motion A, and has No Recommendation on the subject matter of Motion B, for Article 31.
articles.motion_text: Motion A for Changes to ByLaw Article 81 Chemical Storage Tanks and Systems “Move that the Town vote to amend Article 81 of the ByLaws by: adding the words “The word ‘fuel’ in the term ‘underground fuel’ shall be deemed to include propane unless otherwise excepted herein.” at the end of Section 2 (a) so that Section 2 now reads: “Section 2 Applicability a. This By-Law shall apply to all underground fuel or chemical storage tanks and systems. The word ‘fuel’ in the term ‘underground fuel’ shall be deemed to include propane unless otherwise excepted herein. b. Storage systems in service at the time of the approval of this By-Law shall be brought into compliance with the terms of this By-Law within ninety days of its approval.” And by adding the words “This subsection 5 (a) (1) shall not apply to underground propane tanks.” at the end of Section 5 (a) (1) such that Section 5 (a) (1) now reads: “Section 5 Tank Regulations 161 a) Every owner of an underground fuel or chemical storage tank and system shall file with the Board of Health, the size, type, age and location of each tank and/or system and the type of material stored, on or before ninety days after the approval of this By-Law. 1) Thereafter, every owner of a tank and system installation shall comply with the provisions of Section 5A, hereof on or before January 1st of each succeeding year. “This subsection 5 (a) (1) shall not apply to underground propane tanks.” And by adding a new subsection 6 (f) as follows: “f. Subsections 6 (c) , (d) and (e) of this Section 6 shall not apply to underground propane tanks.” And by adding a new subsection i as follows “i. The provisions of subsection 8 (a) (1) and 8 (c) above shall not apply to underground propane tanks.” And by adding to Section 9 Tank Testing and Removal a new subsection (c) as follows: “ c) This Section 9 will not apply to underground propane tanks.” And by adding to (c) as follows: “ c) This Section 10 will not apply to underground propane tanks.” End of Motion A. Motion B: Amending ByLaw Article 22 Town Counsel Move that the town vote to amend Article 22 of the ByLaws by adding a new section 9 as Follows; “Section 9: Special Zoning Advisory Counsel a. The Select Board, or other appointing authority as provided below, shall annually appoint special zoning advisory counsel who shall be legal counsel with specialized knowledge and experience in zoning and land use matters, including but not necessarily be limited to relevant Massachusetts General Laws and case law. In addition to these qualifications said counsel shall be responsible for becoming and remaining fully knowledgeable about the Town’s Zoning ByLaw. Said counsel shall be an independent contractor to the town and may be either associated with or unassociated with Town Counsel. b. Said counsel shall assist and advise i) the Planning Board (including their staff support), ii) the Finance Committee, iii) any standing committee created by representative Town 162 Meeting to review zoning articles and motions, iv) any special zoning study committee of Town Meeting, and v) the Town Moderator if any such enumerated parties request and/or require such assistance or advice concerning proposed changes to the Town’s Zoning ByLaws. Such advice or assistance 1) may consist of analysis, drafting, review and /or explanation of proposed changes to the zoning bylaw and 2) shall be in addition to the role of town counsel. Said zoning advisory counsel shall answer such questions from members of the Representative Town Meeting as the Town Moderator may direct and may also recommend changes to the Planning Board and any committee, established by town meeting, having a responsibility with regard to proposed changes in the Town’s Zoning ByLaw. c. After consideration of a recommendation, if any, that the Planning Board may timely make, such appointment by the Select Board shall be made at least 60 days prior to fiscal year end. In the event the Select Board does not so appoint such special zoning advisory counsel, the Planning Board shall appoint such counsel at least 30 days prior to fiscal year end. If the Planning Board does not so appoint such counsel, the Town Moderator shall make the appointment. The judgement and evaluation of the qualifications for appointing special zoning advisory counsel shall be the decision of the appointing authority. End of Motion B.

For record copy for this warrant article, please see copy on file with Town Clerk of Natick.) For convenience only, a copy of the 4th Edition and the 3rd Edition of Town Meeting Time has been ordered and will be placed on file with the Town Clerk of Natick when the copy arrives. articles.discussion: Paul Griesmer, Chair of the Charter and Bylaw Review Committee (CBRC), presented this article. He explained that our current bylaw references the current edition of “Town Meeting Time”, and there is a new, 4th edition out. The CBRC was asked in July by the Moderator to take a look at the new edition and compare relevant sections in the old version to the new version. The main areas where differences were noted: (1) the new version does not require the seconding of motions. It says if your practice was always to have motions seconded, you 169 should continue that, but if it isn’t seconded, the Moderator “may” allow a motion to die for lack of a second. To make it clear that Town Meeting does want to have motions seconded, the CBRC is proposing adding that to our bylaw. A second is a small but easy check to make sure we aren’t having to debate anything anyone wants to make a motion about. (2) Natick’s current bylaw requires the Finance Committee to hold hearings on each Article that comes before town meeting. That is our current bylaw which was done against the backdrop of the old version which said that if any public hearing is not held, the Moderator should as a matter of procedure, not allow it. However, ⅔ of Town Meeting can waive the hearing requirement. The new edition of “Town Meeting Time” changes it to “a public hearing required by statute” which doesn’t include our bylaw. Finally, the 3rd edition says a Motion to Dissolve is not in order as long as any article is not disposed of. The 4th edition says that the motion to dissolve is not in order for any article that has not been acted upon or passed over, which would be a change from our existing practice. So, we want to preserve our existing practice. Finally, Motion D changes the reference from the floating reference to any edition to a reference to the specific new edition. Questions from the Committee A member asked whether the new approach to dissolving changes the components of de facto dissolution because of failure of quorum. The Sponsor answered “No”, because it only impacts the motion to dissolve, not failure to have a quorum. A member asked what the Moderator’s view is on these suggested changes. Jeff Alderson, Town Moderator, said that he is the one who brought this to the CBRC’s attention. He has read this new edition in detail, and is actually quite happy with a lot of good things that have been added about accessibility and hybrid town meeting - so he’s very excited we’ve made it this far to review this. He thinks the CBRC did a great job with their review and he likes this set of motions. He said the Massachusetts Moderator’s Association suggests adopting the “floating pointer” to the name of the book rather than which edition, but he thinks the proposed approach is better and more cautious. In summary, he supports all four motions. Mr. Alderson further indicated that Town Counsel would also be reviewing it as part of the general review of all articles. Motions and voting: Mr. Jacobs, seconded by Mr. Munnich, moved that we recommend favorable action on the subject matter of Article 32, Motion A, as printed in the meeting materials. The motion passed unanimously (13-0-0). Mr. Jacobs, seconded by Mr. Munnich, moved that we recommend favorable action on the subject matter of Article 32, Motion B, as printed in the meeting materials. The motion passed unanimously (13-0-0). Mr. Jacobs, seconded by Mr. Munnich, moved that we recommend favorable action on the subject matter of Article 32, Motion C, as printed in the meeting materials. The motion 170 passed unanimously (13-0-0). Mr. Jacobs, seconded by Mr. Munnich, moved that we recommend favorable action on the subject matter of Article 32, Motion D, as printed in the meeting materials. The motion passed unanimously (13-0-0). Motion A Redline of Section 2 provided for convenience. Section 2 Motions in Writing All motions require a second when offered for the consideration of the Town Meeting and shall be in writing if required by the Moderator; and all motions involving the expenditure of money shall be in writing when required by any Town Meeting Member. Motion B Redline of Current Section 1 of Article 3 of the Bylaws – for convenience of review only. The first paragraph of current Section 1 of Article 3 of the ByLaws is provided for context only. Only the second paragraph would be changed by Motion B. Section 1 Rules of Procedure The proceedings of Town Meetings shall be governed by such rules of practice as are clearly set out in the most recent edition of Town Meeting Time by Johnson, Trustman and Wadsworth, except as modified by law or by the following sections. In the event that Town Meeting Time does not clearly indicate a single course of action, the ruling of the Moderator shall prevail. Approved by CBRC 5-0-0 on September 9, 2025 In addition to any statutory requirements for public hearings in advance of consideration of articles by Representative Town Meeting, the public hearing held by the Finance Committee on all matters of business within the warrant for any town meeting pursuant to Article 23 Section 4 of these bylaws also constitutes a required mandatory condition precedent to consideration by Representative Town Meeting. When the requirement of a mandatory condition precedent has not met, the Moderator should rule that the motion is not properly before the meeting and that no vote should be taken except as otherwise provided in this Section 1. When mandatory conditions precedent have not been met, allowable motions are ‘postponement to a time certain’ or ‘no action’. In addition, tThe Representative Town Meeting may, by a 2/3 rds vote, waive an requirement in the bylaws for a public hearing, unless required by statute or the Town Charter, on specified or particular matters of business included within the articles of any Town Meeting. Motion C Redline: No redlined text is provided for Motion C because this is an entirely new 3rd paragraph in Section 1 of ByLaw Article 3. Said Section 1 currently consists only of two paragraphs. Motion D Redlined text of the first paragraph of Section 1 of Article 3 provided for convenience. Section 1 Rules of Procedure The proceedings of Town Meetings shall be governed by such rules of practice as are clearly set out in the most recent edition of “Town Meeting Time” by Johnson, Trustman, and 171 Wadsworth, “Town Meeting Time, A Handbook of Parliamentary Law, 4 th Edition” published by the Massachusetts Moderators Association except as modified by law or by the following sections. In the event that “Town Meeting Time” does not clearly indicate a single course of action, the ruling of the Moderator shall prevail. The committee voted 13-0-0 to recommend Favorable Action on the subject matter of Article 32, Motions A, B, C, and D.
articles.motion_text: : Favorable QUANTUM OF VOTE: All Motions: 13-0-0 DATE VOTED: 9/16/2025 MOTION (Requires a Majority Vote) Motion A “Move that the Town vote to amend Section 2 Motions in Writing of Article of Article 3 of the Bylaws by inserting the words “require a second when” after the words “All motions” and before the words “offered for” in the first sentence and by inserting the word “and” after the words “Town Meeting” and before the words “shall be” in the first sentence so that said Section 2 now reads: Section 2 Motions in Writing 167 All motions require a second when offered for the consideration of the Town Meeting and shall be in writing if required by the Moderator; and all motions involving the expenditure of money shall be in writing when required by any Town Meeting Member.” End of Motion A Motion B “Move that the Town vote to amend Section 1 Rules of Procedure of Article 3 of the bylaws by adding the following words to the beginning of the second paragraph of said Section 1: “In addition to any statutory requirements for public hearings in advance of consideration of articles by Representative Town Meeting, the public hearing held by the Finance Committee on all matters of business within the warrant for any town meeting pursuant to Article 23 Section 4 of these bylaws also constitutes a required mandatory condition precedent to consideration by Representative Town Meeting. When mandatory conditions precedent have not been met, allowable motions are postponement to a time certain or no action.” and by inserting the words “In addition,” before the words “The Representative Town Meeting” in the last sentence of the current text of said second paragraph and changing the “T” in the word “The” of said sentence to lower case so that the second paragraph of Section 1 now reads: “In addition to any statutory requirements for public hearings in advance of consideration of articles by Representative Town Meeting, the public hearing held by the Finance Committee on all matters of business within the warrant for any town meeting pursuant to Article 23 Section 4 of these bylaws also constitutes a required mandatory condition precedent to consideration by Representative Town Meeting. When the requirement of a mandatory condition precedent has not met, the Moderator should rule that the motion is not properly before the meeting and that no vote should be taken except as otherwise provided in this Section 1. When mandatory conditions precedent have not been met, allowable motions under these bylaws are ‘postponement to a time certain’ or ‘no action’. In addition, the Representative Town Meeting may, by a 2/3 rds vote, waive any requirement in the bylaws for a public hearing, unless required by statute or the Town Charter, on specified or particular matters of business included within the articles of any Town Meeting.” End of Motion B 168 Motion C “Move that the Town vote to amend Section 1 Rules of Procedure of Article 3 of the Bylaws by adding a new third paragraph to Section 1 Rules of Procedure as follows: The motion to dissolve Representative Town Meeting is not in order as long as any article in the warrant remains undisposed of.” End of Motion C. Motion D “Move that the Town vote to amend the first paragraph of Section 1 Rules of Procedure of Article 3 of the Bylaws by deleting the words “the most recent edition of ‘Town Meeting Time’ by Johnson, Trustman, and Wadsworth” and inserting the words “ Town Meeting Time, A Handbook of Parliamentary Law, 4 th Edition published by the Massachusetts Moderators Association” so that the first paragraph of said Section now reads: Section 1 Rules of Procedure The proceedings of Town Meetings shall be governed by such rules of practice as are clearly set out in “Town Meeting Time, A Handbook of Parliamentary Law, 4 th Edition” published by the Massachusetts Moderators Association except as modified by law or by the following sections. In the event that “Town Meeting Time” does not clearly indicate a single course of action, the ruling of the Moderator shall prevail.” End of Motion D.

articles.article_language: To see if the Town will vote to enact a moratorium on artificial turf fields; or otherwise act thereon.
articles.discussion: Motion A: The Moderator, in consultation with Town Counsel, determined that Motion A is out of scope, and can not be taken up by Town Meeting under this Article’s language. Motion B: Dr. Donna McKenzie, citizen sponsor, presented this article, along with co- sponsors Dr. Roger Luckman, Tien Lum, and Ed Devereaux. They made several points in favor of this advisory resolution:  Some studies have shown negative health impacts related to the microplastics that are used in artificial turf.  These microplastics also create serious environmental concerns, including PFAS contamination of water supplies.  Artificial turf may be more expensive than natural grass when you factor in installation and replacement costs.  This would not prevent the building of a turf field, but rather would allow a “thoughtful pause” so the community as a whole can take a deep look at this issue before deciding how to move forward. A group of members of the public opposed to the moratorium resolution. That group included Julie McDonough, a former school committee member and member of an internal working group created by Natick Public Schools1 to look into this issue. Ms. McDonough made several points against the resolution:  There is a great need for additional field space in Natick.  A turf field will provide year around playability, and many more hours total of usable field time.  A turf field will be less expensive to maintain. Many other communities use turf fields for sports. The process required for building any new field will already be thorough and include review by all the relevant stakeholders. 1 Elise Gorseth, Chair of the School Committee, noted that (as of the date of the Fincom hearing) the School Committee had not yet reviewed this issue. 174 Several members of the public spoke both for and against the proposed resolution, largely mirroring the arguments above. Some also argued that turf fields create more potential injury risk, while others argued that the risk of injury was similar to grass. Motions & Debate Members of the committee made arguments that also largely mirrored those above, with some additional commentary about procedure. Some argued that an advisory moratorium strikes a good balance because it does not require the town to do anything differently but does send a message that the public would like a close look at this before moving forward. However, one member argued that an advisory moratorium was unnecessary because we can have this conversation regardless, as the discussion that evening reflected. The committee voted 6-4-0 on a motion to recommend favorable action on the subject matter of Article 33, Motion B. Because our by-laws require a majority vote of the entire membership of the finance committee (8 out of 15), to make a recommendation, that means the Finance Committee has no recommendation on Article 33, Motion B. The committee voted 10-0-0 to recommend No Action on the subject matter of Article 33 Motion A, and has No Recommendation with regard to Article 33, Motion B.
articles.motion_text: MOTION A Move that Town Meeting adopts a moratorium on artificial turf by adding a new Section # (number of bylaw) to the General Bylaws of the Town of Natick entitled, “Moratorium on Synthetic Artificial Turf,” that reads, “The construction of synthetic or artificial turf on outdoor athletic fields located on Town land shall be prohibited for a period of three years from January 1, 2026-December 31, 2028. This Bylaw shall not apply to the installation or use of synthetic/artificial turf in indoor facilities. An indoor facility is one that has a permanent roof enclosed by permanent non-removable, non-retractable walls and doors. This Bylaw will not affect the use or maintenance of the existing three artificial turf fields at Cole Athletic Field (Operated by Parks and Recreation, 179 Boden Lane), Memorial Field (Natick High School, 92-108 West St.), and Kennedy Middle School (165 Mill St.). This moratorium may be extended upon its expiration by a majority vote of Town Meeting.” MOTION B Move that Town Meeting adopt an advisory for all town departments to abide by a three-year moratorium on the construction of artificial turf fields on town property for a period of three 173 years from January 1, 2026-December 31, 2028. This Advisory shall not apply to the installation or use of synthetic/artificial turf in indoor facilities. An indoor facility is one that has a permanent roof enclosed by permanent non-removable, non-retractable walls and doors. This Advisory will not affect the use or maintenance of the existing three artificial turf fields at Cole Athletic Field (Operated by Parks and Recreation, 179 Boden Lane), Memorial Field (Natick High School, 92-108 West St.), and Kennedy Middle School (165 Mill St.). This Advisory on a moratorium may be extended upon its expiration by a majority vote of Town Meeting.
